column3: Semester 2, 2026 commences on 27 July 2026. The BIT is available for Semester 2 entry, so you can plan your arrival accordingly.

column2: I studied in the UK and completed A-Levels. Are those accepted for entry into the BIT?
column3: Yes. UQ generally accepts UK A-Levels as a recognised secondary qualification for entry. You should confirm the minimum grade requirements and any prerequisite subject conditions by visiting study.uq.edu.au or contacting UQ Admissions.

column2: Are there any mathematics prerequisites I need to meet to get into the BIT as an international student?
column3: Yes. The BIT has a mathematics prerequisite requirement. You should confirm the specific subject and level needed for your qualification type at study.uq.edu.au or by contacting UQ Admissions, as requirements can vary by country.

column2: I'm considering the BE(Hons)/IT dual degree. What is the minimum entry score for that program?
column3: The minimum entry score for the Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) / Information Technology dual degree for Semester 1, 2026 is 84. Entry scores are reviewed annually, so check study.uq.edu.au for the most current information.

column2: Do I need to sit any additional UQ-specific tests as part of my international application for BIT?
column3: UQ does not generally require applicants to sit additional UQ-specific entry tests for the BIT; admission is primarily based on your academic results and English proficiency evidence. However, confirm any specific requirements for your country at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: I completed my schooling in India using the CBSE curriculum. What English grade do I need for BIT entry?
column3: For CBSE applicants, a grade of 65 per cent or better in English satisfies UQ's Minimum English requirement for the BIT. You should also ensure you meet the mathematics prerequisite for your intended program.

column2: Can I apply directly to UQ as an international student, or do I have to go through a third party?
column3: International students can apply directly to UQ online through the UQ application portal at study.uq.edu.au, or alternatively through a UQ-approved agent. Both pathways incur the non-refundable AUD $150 application fee.

column2: I heard the BIT is 3 years full-time. Is that right, or can it take longer?
column3: Yes, the standard BIT is 3 years full-time, requiring 48 units in total. If you study part-time (fewer than 6 units per semester) or take leave, it will extend beyond 3 years.

column2: How many units do I need to take each semester to finish the BIT in 3 years?
column3: You need to complete 48 units over 3 years, which means a standard load of 8 units per semester across 6 semesters. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for your specific major's requirements.

column2: Does the BIT have a single major or can I pick multiple?
column3: The BIT is a single-major program. From 2026, students may only complete 1 major, so if you want to study across multiple disciplines, a dual degree or strategic use of electives may be worth exploring with an academic advisor.

column2: What majors can I choose from in the standalone Bachelor of Information Technology?
column3: The BIT offers a range of majors including areas such as Software Technology, Data Science, and more. Check study.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses for the full and current list of available majors.

column2: If I start in Semester 2, will my BIT still take 3 years to complete?
column3: Yes, the BIT is 48 units regardless of your start semester, so full-time study over 3 years applies whether you commence in Semester 1 (23 Feb 2026) or Semester 2 (27 Jul 2026). Check your study plan on the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au for semester-by-semester sequencing.

column2: Is there a suggested order I should take my BIT courses in, or can I do them in any order?
column3: UQ provides suggested semester-by-semester study plans available through the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au and Programs and Courses. Some courses also have prerequisites, so following the recommended plan helps you progress smoothly.

column2: What is the difference in structure between the BIT and the BIT (Honours)?
column3: The standard BIT is a 3-year, 48-unit program, while the BIT (Honours) is an additional year of advanced study requiring a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale. The Honours year involves a research or advanced project component on top of the undergraduate program.

column2: I want to do Engineering and IT together. How long will that dual degree take?
column3: The Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) / IT is a dual degree and will take longer than the standalone 3-year BIT. For exact duration and structure, check study.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses.

column2: Do the BIT dual degrees still require me to complete a major in both programs?
column3: From 2026, dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program; majors are no longer possible in dual degrees. Confirm the specific structure for your chosen dual degree at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Are elective units part of the BIT, and how many would I have?
column3: Yes, the BIT includes elective units alongside core and major courses within its 48-unit structure. The exact number of electives depends on your chosen major; check Programs and Courses for the breakdown.

column2: Can I take courses outside of IT as part of my BIT electives?
column3: BIT students may be able to use elective units to take courses from other disciplines, depending on availability and any prerequisites. Consult Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au or seek academic advice to confirm your options.

column2: What topics does the BIT actually cover? I'm not sure if it's just coding.
column3: The BIT covers a broad range of areas including social and mobile computing, software development, data science, human-computer interaction, and web design — it goes well beyond just coding. Check study.uq.edu.au for the full curriculum overview.

column2: If I add the Concurrent Diploma in Languages to my BIT, how many extra units does that add?
column3: The Concurrent Diploma in Languages is 16 units, studied alongside your BIT. It is designed to complement your degree without significantly extending your total study time.

column2: Is the BIT accredited by any professional body?
column3: Yes, the Bachelor of Information Technology is accredited by the Australian Computer Society (ACS), which is recognised nationally and contributes to the international recognition of your qualification.

column2: What GPA do I need to get into BIT Honours, and when do I apply?
column3: You need a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale to apply for BIT (Honours). Application deadlines are: Semester 1 — international 30 Nov, domestic 31 Jan; Semester 2 — international 31 May, domestic 30 Jun.

column2: Is the BIT/Arts dual degree structured differently now compared to previous years?
column3: Yes. From 2026, the BIT/Arts dual degree does not include an IT major, and dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program. Check study.uq.edu.au for the current structure.

column2: How does part-time study affect my BIT program timeline?
column3: Part-time study is defined as fewer than 6 units per semester (less than 75% of a full-time load), and will extend the BIT beyond the standard 3 years. Note that international students on a student visa are generally required to study full-time.

column2: Where can I find the semester-by-semester course plan for my specific BIT major?
column3: Study plans showing the suggested semester-by-semester course sequence are available on the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au and through Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Does the BIT include any industry experience or work placements as part of the program?
column3: UQ supports employability through programs like Enhance Your Employability, which includes internships and industry events relevant to IT students. For details on whether a formal placement is embedded in your specific major, check study.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses.

column2: What is the standard full-time study load per semester in the BIT?
column3: The standard full-time load is 8 units per semester. Over 6 semesters across 3 years, this adds up to the 48 units required to graduate from the BIT.

column2: I want to finish my BIT as fast as possible — is there a way to complete it in less than 3 years?
column3: The BIT is a 3-year program requiring 48 units. Accelerated completion may be possible through credit transfer for prior study, but you should contact UQ Admissions to confirm what credit you may be eligible for.

column2: How many courses would I normally take each semester in the BIT?
column3: A standard full-time load is 4 courses (8 units) per semester, which means 8 courses per year across two semesters.

column2: If I start the BIT in Semester 2, will I still finish in 3 years?
column3: Yes, provided you maintain a full-time load of 8 units per semester. Semester 2, 2026 commences 27 July 2026, and you would still complete the required 48 units over 3 years.

column2: What majors can I choose in the standalone BIT?
column3: The BIT offers majors in areas such as Computer Science, Data Science, Information Systems, and Software Engineering. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the full current list of available majors.

column2: Does the BIT have a set list of compulsory courses or can I choose everything myself?
column3: The BIT includes a combination of compulsory core courses and elective units. The exact breakdown depends on your chosen major; refer to your study plan on the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au.

column2: What if I want to do the BIT but also get an engineering qualification — is there a dual degree for that?
column3: Yes. UQ offers a Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) / Information Technology dual degree. International students can also apply for this program.

column2: How long does the BE(Hons)/IT dual degree take to complete?
column3: The BE(Hons)/IT is a longer program than the standalone BIT due to the added Engineering (Honours) component. For the exact duration and unit requirements, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: I heard dual degree students can only do one major now — is that true?
column3: Yes. From 2026, dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program; majors are no longer possible in dual degrees.

column2: Does the BIT include any practical or industry experience as part of the program structure?
column3: The BIT curriculum includes areas such as industry-relevant projects and teamwork. For details on formal industry placement or internship components, check the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au.

column2: Can I switch from the standard BIT to the BIT (Honours) partway through my degree?
column3: You can apply for the BIT (Honours) after meeting the minimum GPA requirement of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale. Application deadlines are 30 November (S1 international), 31 January (S1 domestic), 31 May (S2 international), and 30 June (S2 domestic).

column2: If I study part-time in my first year and then full-time after that, will it still take 3 years total?
column3: No. Any period of part-time study will extend your completion time beyond the standard 3 years, since part-time means fewer than 6 units per semester and the total 48 units still need to be completed.

column2: Does the BIT have a capstone project or final-year project built into the structure?
column3: Many IT programs at UQ include a final-year project or capstone experience. For specifics on whether your chosen major includes this, refer to the study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses.

column2: Is the BIT/Arts dual degree structured with an IT major from 2026?
column3: No. From 2026, the BIT/Arts dual degree does not include an IT major. Students may complete one major toward the single program within the dual degree.

column2: How is the BIT structured differently from the BCom/IT dual degree?
column3: The standalone BIT is a 3-year, 48-unit program focused on IT, while the BCom/IT combines IT with Commerce subjects including majors in Accounting, Business Analytics, Business Information Systems, and Finance. The dual degree is longer due to the added Commerce requirements.

column2: Are there elective units in the BIT where I can study something outside of IT?
column3: Yes, the BIT includes elective units that can allow study outside your core IT major. For guidance on how to use electives, refer to your study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au or seek academic advice.

column2: What year-level courses am I expected to take first in the BIT — is there a recommended order?
column3: Yes. Semester-by-semester course sequences are outlined in study plans available from the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au and through Programs and Courses. Following these plans helps ensure you meet prerequisites in the correct order.

column2: I already have a diploma in IT — could I get credit towards the BIT and skip some first-year courses?
column3: You may be eligible for credit transfer depending on your prior qualification, which could reduce the units you need to complete. Contact UQ Admissions to assess your eligibility before applying.

column2: Does the BIT program run in both Semester 1 and Semester 2, or only Semester 1?
column3: The BIT is available for both Semester 1 (commencing 23 February 2026) and Semester 2 (commencing 27 July 2026) intakes.

column2: What areas of IT will I actually be studying across the 3 years of the BIT?
column3: The BIT covers areas including social and mobile computing, software development, data science, information systems, and human-computer interaction, among others depending on your major. Refer to Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the full curriculum detail.

column2: I heard the BIT is a 3-year degree — does that mean I finish in 3 years no matter what?
column3: The BIT is designed to be completed in 3 years of full-time study, totalling 48 units. If you study part-time or fail courses, your completion time will extend beyond 3 years.

column2: How many units do I need to take each semester to finish BIT on time?
column3: To complete the BIT in 3 years, you need to study 8 units per semester across 6 semesters, which is the standard full-time load of 16 units per year.

column2: What is the difference between the BIT and the BIT (Honours)?
column3: The Bachelor of Information Technology is a standard 3-year undergraduate degree totalling 48 units, while the BIT (Honours) is an additional honours year requiring a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale. They are distinct programs with separate entry requirements.

column2: If I want to do Honours after my BIT, what GPA do I need?
column3: You need a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale to be eligible for the BIT (Honours) program.

column2: Does the BIT have a fixed structure or can I choose my own courses?
column3: The BIT has a structured curriculum that includes compulsory core courses, a major, and elective units. Suggested semester-by-semester study plans are available from the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au and through Programs and Courses.

column2: What majors can I choose from in the standalone BIT?
column3: The BIT offers a range of majors including areas such as computer science, data science, software engineering, and others. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the full and current list of available majors.

column2: Can I switch majors after I start my BIT, or am I locked in from day one?
column3: You are not necessarily locked in from day one, but changing your major later may affect your study plan and completion timeline. It is best to seek academic advice from EECS or UQ Student Services as early as possible if you are considering a change.

column2: I want to start in Semester 2 — is the full BIT program available then?
column3: Yes, the BIT is available for both Semester 1 (commencing 23 February 2026) and Semester 2 (commencing 27 July 2026) intakes. Check study.uq.edu.au to confirm availability for your chosen major and intake.

column2: Does the BIT include any compulsory work experience or internship as part of the degree?
column3: The BIT curriculum includes industry-relevant coursework and UQ provides employability programs including internship opportunities, but whether a formal internship is a compulsory component of your specific major should be confirmed at study.uq.edu.au or with EECS.

column2: If I fail a course in first year, will it push back my whole degree significantly?
column3: Failing a course can affect your study plan, particularly if that course is a prerequisite for later courses, potentially extending your overall completion time. You should seek academic advice from EECS to adjust your study plan accordingly.

column2: How many units is the BIT (Honours) year on top of the standard BIT?
column3: The BIT (Honours) is a separate program undertaken after completing your BIT. For the exact unit count for the honours year, refer to Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: When do I need to apply for BIT Honours if I want to start in Semester 1?
column3: For a Semester 1 start, the application deadline is 30 November for international students and 31 January for domestic students.

column2: I'm thinking of doing a dual degree — how long will that take compared to the standalone BIT?
column3: Dual degrees combine two programs and typically take longer than the standalone 3-year BIT. The exact duration depends on which dual degree you choose, so check the specific program details at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: In a dual degree with BIT, how many majors can I complete from 2026 onward?
column3: From 2026, dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program component; majors are no longer possible within dual degrees.

column2: What's the standard full-time study load per year in the BIT?
column3: The standard full-time load is 16 units per year, typically structured as 8 units per semester across two semesters.

column2: Does the BIT/Arts dual degree include an IT major from 2026?
column3: No. From 2026, the BIT/Arts dual degree does not include an IT major. Dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program component.

column2: What happens to my program structure if I take a leave of absence for a semester?
column3: Taking leave will extend your overall completion time beyond the standard 3 years. You should notify UQ and seek academic advice to understand how leave affects your enrolment, fees, and study plan before proceeding.

column2: Is the BIT structured the same way for both domestic and international students?
column3: Yes, the program structure — including the 48-unit requirement, majors, and core courses — is the same for both domestic and international students, though some administrative processes such as fees and visa conditions differ.

column2: Can I study the BIT part-time if I'm an international student on a student visa?
column3: Student visa conditions generally require international students to maintain a full-time enrolment. You should contact UQ International or the Department of Home Affairs to understand how part-time study may affect your visa.

column2: Are there any courses in the BIT that run across a full year rather than a single semester?
column3: Most UQ courses are delivered within a single semester. For specific course delivery details within your BIT major, refer to Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au or contact EECS directly.

column2: I heard the BIT is a 3-year degree — does that mean I finish after 6 semesters if I study full-time?
column3: Yes. Full-time students complete the BIT in 6 semesters (3 years), finishing 48 units in total. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the recommended semester-by-semester study plan.

column2: What does a normal full-time semester look like for a BIT student in terms of how many courses I'd be taking?
column3: A standard full-time load is 8 units per semester, which typically means 4 courses of 2 units each. Over two semesters per year that gives you 16 units annually, totalling 48 units across the 3-year program.

column2: Is the BIT structured around one main specialisation or can I mix and match different IT areas?
column3: The BIT is a single-major program, so you complete one major as your primary specialisation. You can use elective units to explore other IT areas, but a formal second major is not accommodated within the standard 48-unit structure.

column2: What majors are actually available if I enrol in the standalone BIT?
column3: The BIT offers majors across key IT disciplines including areas such as Software Engineering, Computer Science, Data Science, and more. Check study.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses for the full current list of available majors.

column2: If I start in Semester 2, does that change how long the BIT takes to complete?
column3: No. Regardless of whether you commence in Semester 1 (23 Feb 2026) or Semester 2 (27 Jul 2026), the BIT still requires 48 units over 3 years of full-time study. Your study plan will simply be offset by one semester.

column2: Does the BIT have a core set of compulsory courses everyone has to do, or is it mostly electives?
column3: The BIT includes both compulsory core courses and major-specific courses, as well as some elective units. The exact breakdown depends on your chosen major; refer to the study plans at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses.

column2: What if I want to do Honours after finishing the standard BIT — does that add extra time to my degree?
column3: Yes. The BIT (Honours) is a separate one-year program undertaken after completing the standard BIT, so it extends your total study by one year. You need a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale to be eligible.

column2: I'm considering the BE(Hons)/IT dual degree — how does the program length compare to just doing the BIT alone?
column3: The BE(Hons)/IT is a longer dual degree compared to the standalone 3-year BIT. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the exact duration and unit requirements of the dual degree.

column2: Are there any capstone or industry project courses built into the BIT program?
column3: The BIT curriculum incorporates project-based and industry-relevant learning; however, the specific capstone or project courses depend on your major. Check the relevant study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au for details.

column2: Does the BIT program follow a semester system or a trimester system?
column3: UQ operates on a semester system. The BIT has two main intakes per year: Semester 1 commencing 23 February 2026 and Semester 2 commencing 27 July 2026.

column2: If I fail a course and have to repeat it, will that push my graduation past 3 years?
column3: Yes. If you need to repeat a course, you will need to complete additional units beyond your standard semester load, which can extend your study beyond the standard 3 years. Seek academic advice from EECS if this situation arises.

column2: Can I take more than 8 units in a semester to try to finish the BIT faster?
column3: Overloading beyond the standard 8 units per semester is subject to academic approval and eligibility criteria. Contact UQ's EECS school or student services to discuss whether an overload is possible in your situation.

column2: What areas of IT does the BIT cover throughout the program — like, is it just coding or is there more to it?
column3: The BIT covers a broad range of areas including software development, data science, social and mobile computing, cybersecurity, human-computer interaction, and web design, among others. The depth in each area depends on your chosen major.

column2: Is there a work-integrated learning or internship component built into the BIT?
column3: UQ provides career development opportunities including internships and industry events through its Enhance Your Employability program and EAIT industry connections. Whether a formal internship is embedded in your specific major's structure should be confirmed at eecs.uq.edu.au.

column2: Does the BIT program have any mathematics or science requirements built into the structure, or is it purely IT?
column3: The BIT has mathematics prerequisites for entry, and quantitative or technical courses are typically part of the curriculum depending on your major. Check the study plan for your chosen major at eecs.uq.edu.au for specifics.

column2: What's the difference in structure between the BIT and the BIT (Honours)?
column3: The standard BIT is a 3-year, 48-unit program with one major. The BIT (Honours) is an additional one-year program for graduates who meet the minimum 5.0 GPA on UQ's 7-point scale, providing a higher-level qualification with research or advanced coursework components.

column2: If I'm doing a BIT dual degree, do I still get to complete a major in the IT component?
column3: From 2026, dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program; majors are no longer possible in the dual degree component. Confirm the structure of your specific dual degree at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: How is the BIT/Arts dual degree structured — do I do the two degrees side by side or one after the other?
column3: In a dual degree, both programs are studied concurrently, meaning you take courses from both the BIT and Arts components across your semesters. Note that from 2026, the BIT/Arts does not include an IT major, and only 1 major from the Arts component is completed.

column2: Are the courses in the BIT the same regardless of which major I pick, at least in first year?
column3: First-year BIT students typically share a set of common core courses before specialising into their chosen major in later years. Check the study plans at eecs.uq.edu.au for the exact first-year course requirements for your intended major.

column2: Can I transfer credit from a previous IT diploma or TAFE qualification to reduce how long I spend completing the BIT?
column3: Credit transfer or recognition of prior learning may be available depending on your previous qualification. Submit a credit application through UQ's admissions process; visit study.uq.edu.au for guidance on how to apply for credit transfer.

column2: I keep hearing about 'units' at UQ — how many do I actually need to finish the BIT?
column3: You need to complete 48 units in total to graduate from the 3-year Bachelor of Information Technology. A standard full-time load is 8 units per semester, so that works out to 6 semesters over 3 years.

column2: If I study full-time, how many courses am I typically taking each semester in the BIT?
column3: A standard full-time load is 8 units per semester. Most BIT courses are 2 units each, so you would typically enrol in 4 courses per semester.

column2: Does the BIT have a fixed structure or can I choose most of my courses freely?
column3: The BIT has a combination of required core courses, major courses, and elective units. The exact breakdown depends on your chosen major and commencement year, so check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the full structure.

column2: What majors can I actually choose from in the standalone BIT program?
column3: The BIT offers majors including Computer Science, Software Engineering, Information Systems, Data Science, and others. Check study.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses for the full and current list of available majors.

column2: I want to do BIT but I'm worried 3 years isn't enough time — can I extend it?
column3: Yes. You can study part-time (fewer than 6 units per semester), which will extend the program beyond the standard 3 years. Keep in mind that international students on a student visa generally need to maintain a full-time load, so check your visa conditions if applicable.

column2: Is the BIT a single-major or double-major degree?
column3: The standard 3-year BIT is a single-major program. From 2026, students may only complete 1 major toward the single program, so a formal second major is not available within the standard 48 units.

column2: What if I want to do both IT and another field — do I have to do a dual degree, or is there another option?
column3: A dual degree is the most structured way to combine IT with another discipline. Alternatively, you could use elective units strategically to explore other areas, but for a formal qualification in both fields, a dual degree is the appropriate pathway. Seek academic advice to find the best fit.

column2: How long does the BE(Hons)/IT dual degree actually take to complete?
column3: The Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) / Information Technology is a 5-year dual degree program. Check study.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses for the exact unit requirements and structure.

column2: Does the BIT have any work-integrated learning or industry placement built into the structure?
column3: The BIT curriculum includes opportunities related to employability through the Enhance Your Employability program, industry connections, and relevant coursework. For specific details on any mandatory or optional placement components, check study.uq.edu.au or contact the School of EECS.

column2: If I start in Semester 2, will I still be able to follow the normal study plan and graduate in 3 years?
column3: A Semester 2 start is possible, but course availability and prerequisite sequencing may differ from a Semester 1 start. It is recommended to check the study plans at eecs.uq.edu.au and seek academic advice to confirm you can complete all requirements within your intended timeframe.

column2: Can I take more than 8 units in a semester to finish the BIT faster?
column3: Overloading beyond the standard 8 units per semester is possible in some cases but requires approval. Contact Student Services or your academic advisor to find out if you are eligible and what the approval process involves.

column2: Is the Honours version of the BIT a separate 4-year program, or is it something I add on after?
column3: The BIT (Honours) is a separate 1-year program you apply for after completing the standard 3-year BIT. You need a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale to be eligible to apply.

column2: What's the difference in length between the BIT/Arts dual degree and the standalone BIT?
column3: The standalone BIT takes 3 years full-time, while the BIT/Arts dual degree takes 4 years full-time. The extra year accommodates the Arts component of the dual degree.

column2: Do all IT dual degrees take the same amount of time to complete, or does it vary?
column3: Duration varies by dual degree combination. For example, the BIT/Arts takes 4 years, while the BE(Hons)/IT takes 5 years. Check study.uq.edu.au for the specific duration of each dual degree program.

column2: From 2026, how many majors can I complete if I'm in a BIT dual degree?
column3: From 2026, dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program; majors are no longer possible in dual degrees. This means you complete one IT major as part of your dual qualification.

column2: Where can I find the recommended course order for each year of the BIT?
column3: Study plans showing the suggested semester-by-semester course sequence are available from the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au and through Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: I'm thinking about the BBusMgt/IT — how many years is that compared to the standalone BIT?
column3: The Bachelor of Business Management / Information Technology is a 4-year dual degree, compared to 3 years for the standalone BIT. The extra year covers the Business Management component of the program.

column2: What happens to my program structure if I take a leave of absence during the BIT?
column3: Taking a leave of absence pauses your studies but does not change the total requirements to graduate. However, course availability and program rules may change during your absence, so it is important to seek academic advice before and after your leave at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Are there any required first-year core courses that every BIT student has to do regardless of their major?
column3: Yes, the BIT has core courses that all students complete in addition to major-specific courses. The exact list of core requirements depends on your commencement year and major, so refer to Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the specific requirements.

column2: I heard the BIT is 3 years full-time — is that right, or can it be longer?
column3: Yes, the standard BIT is 3 years full-time, requiring 48 units in total. If you study part-time (fewer than 6 units per semester), the degree will take longer than 3 years.

column2: How many units would I typically enrol in each semester if I'm doing the BIT full-time?
column3: A standard full-time load is 8 units per semester, which equals 16 units per year. Over 3 years, this adds up to the 48 units required to graduate.

column2: What kind of things will I actually study in the BIT? Like what are the main areas covered?
column3: The BIT covers areas including social and mobile computing, web design and development, and other core IT disciplines. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the full list of courses and majors available.

column2: Does the BIT have majors I can specialise in, or is it all the same for everyone?
column3: Yes, the BIT offers majors that allow you to specialise within the program. You can complete one major as part of the standard 3-year, 48-unit degree.

column2: If I start in Semester 2, will my BIT still take the same amount of time to finish?
column3: Yes, the BIT still requires 48 units regardless of whether you commence in Semester 1 (23 Feb 2026) or Semester 2 (27 Jul 2026), so the total duration remains 3 years at full-time load.

column2: Is the BIT accredited by any professional body, or is it just a regular university degree?
column3: Yes, the BIT is accredited by the Australian Computer Society (ACS), which is a recognised professional body for IT in Australia.

column2: What's the difference between the BIT and the BIT Honours? Are they separate programs?
column3: Yes, they are separate programs. The standard BIT is a 3-year undergraduate degree, while the BIT (Honours) is an additional research-focused year typically undertaken after completing the BIT, with a minimum GPA requirement of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale.

column2: I want to do Honours after my BIT — what GPA do I need and when do I apply?
column3: You need a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale to be eligible for BIT (Honours). Application deadlines are 30 Nov (S1 international), 31 Jan (S1 domestic), 31 May (S2 international), and 30 Jun (S2 domestic).

column2: Can I do a dual degree with BIT and still finish in 3 years?
column3: No, BIT dual degrees are longer than the standard 3-year single degree because you are completing the requirements of two programs. The exact duration depends on the specific dual degree combination.

column2: In a BIT dual degree, how many majors can I complete from 2026 onwards?
column3: From 2026, dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program component; majors are no longer possible within the dual degree structure itself.

column2: Does the BIT/Arts dual degree include an IT major I can choose?
column3: No. From 2026, the BIT/Arts dual degree does not include an IT major. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the current structure.

column2: What if I want to take a break mid-degree — will that affect my total program duration?
column3: Yes, taking a leave of absence or reducing your study load will extend the time it takes to complete the 48 units required to graduate. You should contact UQ Student Services for advice on how a leave of absence may affect your enrolment.

column2: Are there elective units in the BIT, or is every course compulsory?
column3: The BIT includes a combination of compulsory core courses and elective units. The specific breakdown depends on your chosen major; refer to the study plans at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses for details.

column2: I'm thinking about adding a Diploma in Languages to my BIT — will that make the degree take longer?
column3: The Concurrent Diploma in Languages is 16 units and is designed to be studied alongside your BIT without significantly extending your total study time. You can apply for it after enrolling in the BIT.

column2: Where can I find a semester-by-semester plan for what courses to take in the BIT?
column3: Study plans showing the suggested semester-by-semester course sequence are available from the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au and through UQ's Programs and Courses.

column2: Does the BIT have any work-integrated learning or industry placements built into the program structure?
column3: UQ provides employability support through programs like Enhance Your Employability, including internships and industry events relevant to IT students. For details on whether placements are formally embedded in your specific major, check Programs and Courses or contact EECS at eecs.uq.edu.au.

column2: If I fail a course, does that mean I'll need an extra semester to graduate?
column3: Failing a course means you will need to retake it to meet the 48-unit graduation requirement, which could extend your study duration. You should seek academic advice through UQ Student Services to understand your options.

column2: Is CSSE1001 one of the first courses I'd take in the BIT, and what leads on from it?
column3: CSSE1001 is a foundational programming course, and completing it (or ENGG1001) is a prerequisite for CSSE2002 Programming in the Large, which is a more advanced course in the BIT program.

column2: I noticed DECO1400 is listed in the BIT — is web design a core part of the program or just an elective?
column3: DECO1400 Introduction to Web Design is part of the BIT curriculum, covering web design, human-centred design, and basic scripting. Whether it is a core or elective requirement depends on your major; consult the study plans at eecs.uq.edu.au for confirmation.

column2: Can I speed up my BIT by taking extra courses each semester and finish in under 3 years?
column3: Taking a heavier course load may allow you to complete the 48 required units faster, but exceeding the standard 8 units per semester typically requires faculty approval. Contact UQ Student Services or the EECS faculty to discuss your options.

column2: I keep hearing about 'units' at UQ. How many units is a full-time semester load for the BIT?
column3: A standard full-time semester load is 8 units, which typically means 4 courses each worth 2 units. Over a full year, that equals 16 units.

column2: If I start BIT in Semester 2, 2026, when would I expect to finish if I study full-time the whole way through?
column3: Semester 2, 2026 commences on 27 July 2026. Studying full-time at 16 units per year, you would complete the 48-unit BIT after 3 years, finishing around mid-2029.

column2: Does the BIT have a work integrated learning or internship component built into the program?
column3: The BIT curriculum includes industry-relevant projects and UQ provides employability programs with internship opportunities through the Enhance Your Employability program. Check study.uq.edu.au or the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au for specific course-level details.

column2: Is the BIT only offered at one campus or can I study it at multiple UQ campuses?
column3: The BIT is primarily offered at UQ's St Lucia campus. Confirm current campus availability at study.uq.edu.au as delivery arrangements can change.

column2: What's the difference between the BIT and the BIT (Honours)? Are they separate programs?
column3: Yes, they are separate programs. The standard BIT is a 3-year, 48-unit degree, while the BIT (Honours) is an additional year of advanced study requiring a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale and a separate application.

column2: I want to do BIT Honours. What GPA do I need and are there application deadlines I should know about?
column3: You need a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale. Application deadlines are: Semester 1 — international students by 30 November, domestic students by 31 January; Semester 2 — international students by 31 May, domestic students by 30 June.

column2: How many courses would I typically take per semester in the BIT?
column3: A standard full-time student takes 4 courses per semester, with each course worth 2 units, totalling 8 units per semester.

column2: Does the BIT program have elective courses or is everything compulsory?
column3: The BIT includes both core compulsory courses and elective units that allow you to tailor your studies. The specific breakdown depends on your chosen major; refer to the study plans at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses for details.

column2: If I do a dual degree like BIT/Arts, how long will that take compared to the standalone BIT?
column3: Dual degrees are longer than the 3-year standalone BIT because you are completing requirements for two programs. The exact duration depends on the specific dual degree; check study.uq.edu.au for the unit count and expected duration of your chosen combination.

column2: From 2026, can I complete two IT majors within a BIT dual degree?
column3: No. From 2026, dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program, so majors are no longer possible in dual degrees.

column2: What does the BIT curriculum actually cover? Like what kinds of topics will I be studying?
column3: The BIT covers areas including programming, software engineering, computer science, web design, social and mobile computing, and more, depending on your chosen major. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for a full curriculum breakdown.

column2: Is the BIT accredited by any professional body, or is it just a university degree?
column3: Yes, the BIT is accredited by the Australian Computer Society (ACS), which gives it professional recognition within the Australian IT industry and internationally.

column2: What if I fail a course — does that mean I'll definitely take longer than 3 years to finish the BIT?
column3: Failing a course may require you to repeat it, which could affect your progression and potentially extend your time beyond the standard 3 years. Seek academic advice from your faculty if this occurs.

column2: I'm thinking about the BE(Hons)/IT dual degree. Does that degree also follow the same 48-unit structure as the standalone BIT?
column3: No. The BE(Hons)/IT is a dual degree with a different and longer structure than the standalone 48-unit BIT. Check study.uq.edu.au for the specific unit requirements and duration of the BE(Hons)/IT.

column2: Does the BIT have a set sequence I have to follow, or can I take courses in any order I want?
column3: Some BIT courses have prerequisites that must be completed first, so you cannot take all courses in any order. Suggested semester-by-semester study plans are available at eecs.uq.edu.au to help you plan your sequence correctly.

column2: What's the earliest semester I can commence the BIT in 2026?
column3: The earliest intake is Semester 1, 2026, which commences on 23 February 2026.

column2: I've heard some degrees at UQ have a mid-year intake. Does the BIT offer that?
column3: Yes, the BIT offers both Semester 1 and Semester 2 intakes. Semester 2, 2026 commences on 27 July 2026.

column2: If I'm doing the BIT and want to also add a Concurrent Diploma in Languages, will that make my degree longer?
column3: The Concurrent Diploma in Languages is 16 units and is designed to be studied alongside your bachelor's degree without significantly extending your total study time, though it does add to your overall unit load each semester.

column2: I'm thinking of doing the BIT but I'm worried it might take longer than 3 years. How long does it normally take to finish?
column3: The standard BIT is a 3-year full-time program requiring 48 units in total. If you study a full load of 8 units per semester, you will complete it within 3 years.

column2: What does a typical semester look like in terms of how many courses I'd be taking?
column3: A standard full-time load is 8 units per semester (16 units per year). Most courses are 2 units each, so you would typically take 4 courses per semester.

column2: If I take a semester off during my BIT, will that push my graduation back?
column3: Yes. Taking a semester off means you will need additional semesters to complete the required 48 units, extending your degree beyond the standard 3 years. Contact UQ Student Services to discuss leave of absence options and the impact on your study plan.

column2: Does the BIT have a major I need to choose, or is the whole degree one general program?
column3: The BIT requires you to complete one major as part of the 48-unit program. Available majors include areas such as Computer Science, Data Science, and Software Engineering — check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the full current list.

column2: I heard some degrees at UQ have an Honours year. Does the BIT have one and does it add extra time?
column3: Yes, the BIT (Honours) is a separate program that follows the standard BIT and requires a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale to be eligible. It adds an additional year of study beyond the 3-year BIT.

column2: What if I want to do a BIT dual degree — how much longer would that take compared to just doing the BIT on its own?
column3: BIT dual degrees typically take 4 to 5 years depending on the paired program, compared to 3 years for the standalone BIT. The exact duration depends on which dual degree you choose, so check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the specific program length.

column2: Are there any core courses everyone in the BIT has to do, or is it all electives?
column3: Yes, the BIT includes compulsory core courses that all students must complete, alongside major-specific courses and elective units. The exact requirements depend on your major and commencement year, so refer to your study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses.

column2: Can I start my BIT in Semester 2 or is it only available from Semester 1?
column3: The BIT is available for both Semester 1 and Semester 2 intakes. Semester 1, 2026 commences 23 February 2026, and Semester 2, 2026 commences 27 July 2026.

column2: I'm an international student — does full-time study mean something different for visa purposes compared to the general program structure?
column3: For visa purposes, international students on a student visa are generally required to maintain a full-time enrolment, which aligns with the standard 8 units per semester load in the BIT. You should confirm your specific visa conditions with UQ International or the Department of Home Affairs.

column2: Is the structure of the BIT the same for domestic and international students, or are there different requirements?
column3: The academic program structure — 48 units over 3 years with one major — is the same for both domestic and international students. The main differences relate to tuition fees, application processes, and visa requirements rather than the program content itself.

column2: What majors can I choose from in the BIT at UQ?
column3: The BIT offers majors in Computer Science, Data Science, Information Systems, Information Technology, and Software Engineering. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the full list and any updates for your commencement year.

column2: I'm really into building apps and software — which BIT major would suit me best?
column3: The Software Engineering major is likely the best fit, as it focuses on the design, development, and maintenance of software systems. The Computer Science major may also be relevant depending on the depth of technical theory you want.

column2: Does the BIT have a cybersecurity major?
column3: A standalone cybersecurity major is not listed among the confirmed BIT majors. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the current list, as offerings can change annually.

column2: What is the Data Science major in the BIT actually about?
column3: The Data Science major focuses on analysing and interpreting large datasets, covering areas such as machine learning, statistical modelling, and data-driven decision making. It is suited to students interested in working with data in business or research contexts.

column2: Is the Information Systems major the same as Information Technology?
column3: No, they are distinct majors. Information Systems tends to focus on how technology is used within organisations and business processes, while Information Technology covers broader technical and computing skills. Review the course lists for each major at study.uq.edu.au to find the best fit.

column2: Can I switch my BIT major after I've already started the degree?
column3: It may be possible to change your major after commencing, but this depends on your completed courses and whether they satisfy the new major's requirements. Contact UQ's Student Services or your academic advisor for guidance specific to your situation.

column2: If I'm doing the BIT/Arts dual degree, do I still pick an IT major?
column3: No. From 2026, the BIT/Arts dual degree does not include an IT major. Students in dual degrees may only complete 1 major toward the single program, and majors are no longer possible in the dual degree component.

column2: What majors are available in the BIT within the BE(Hons)/IT dual degree?
column3: From 2026, dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program, meaning majors are no longer possible in the dual degree component. Check study.uq.edu.au for the current structure of the BE(Hons)/IT.

column2: I want to work in AI — is there a major in the BIT that covers artificial intelligence?
column3: While there is no confirmed standalone AI major in the BIT, topics related to artificial intelligence and machine learning are covered within the Data Science and Computer Science majors. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for specific course offerings.

column2: Does the BBusMgt/IT dual degree let me pick an IT major?
column3: From 2026, dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program, so majors are no longer available in the dual degree component. Refer to study.uq.edu.au for the current program structure.

column2: What IT majors can I choose from in the BCom/IT dual degree?
column3: From 2026, IT majors are no longer available in dual degrees. The Commerce component of the BCom/IT offers majors in Accounting, Business Analytics, Business Information Systems, and Finance. Check study.uq.edu.au for the latest program details.

column2: Is the Computer Science major in the BIT accredited by the ACS?
column3: Yes. The BIT as a whole is accredited by the Australian Computer Society (ACS), which covers the program regardless of your chosen major. This accreditation is internationally recognised.

column2: How do I decide which BIT major to choose if I'm not sure yet?
column3: It is worth reviewing the course lists and study plans for each major at eecs.uq.edu.au and Programs and Courses, as well as considering your career interests. You can also seek academic advice through UQ Student Services to help match a major to your goals.

column2: Can I complete more than one major in the standalone 3-year BIT?
column3: The standard 3-year BIT is a single-major program with 48 units, and its structure does not typically accommodate a formal second major. If you want broader coverage, consider a dual degree or discuss elective options with an academic advisor.

column2: What is the Software Engineering major in the BIT focused on?
column3: The Software Engineering major focuses on the systematic approach to designing, developing, testing, and maintaining software systems at scale. It is suited to students aiming for careers in software development and engineering roles.

column2: Does the Information Technology major in the BIT differ from the overall degree name?
column3: Yes. The Information Technology major is one of several specialisations available within the Bachelor of Information Technology degree, with its own specific course requirements. Review the major's course list at study.uq.edu.au to understand how it differs from other majors like Computer Science or Software Engineering.

column2: I'm interested in how businesses use technology — which major should I look at?
column3: The Information Systems major is likely the most relevant, as it focuses on the intersection of technology and organisational processes. You might also consider the BCom/IT or BBusMgt/IT dual degrees if you want a stronger business component.

column2: Are the BIT majors the same across all dual degrees, or do they vary?
column3: From 2026, majors are no longer available in any BIT dual degree; students may only complete 1 major toward the single program component. Consult study.uq.edu.au for the specific structure of each dual degree program.

column2: Do I need to choose my BIT major before I start, or can I decide later?
column3: You do not necessarily need to finalise your major before commencing, as some core courses are shared across majors in early semesters. However, you should confirm the decision timeline with UQ Admissions or your academic advisor, as choosing late may affect your study plan.

column2: What is the difference between the Computer Science and Software Engineering majors in the BIT?
column3: Computer Science tends to focus more on computational theory, algorithms, and foundational principles, while Software Engineering emphasises the practical processes of building and managing large-scale software systems. Review the specific course requirements for each major at Programs and Courses to identify which aligns better with your interests.

column2: What majors are available in the standalone Bachelor of Information Technology at UQ?
column3: The BIT offers majors including Computer Science, Data Science, Information Systems, Information Technology, and Software Engineering. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the full and current list of available majors.

column2: I want to study both Software Engineering and Data Science in my BIT. Can I do two majors?
column3: The standard 3-year BIT is a single-major program, so its 48-unit structure does not typically accommodate a formal second major. If you want to combine specialisations, consider a dual degree or discuss elective options with an academic advisor.

column2: What IT majors can I choose in the BIT/Arts dual degree?
column3: From 2026, the BIT/Arts dual degree does not include an IT major component. Students may only complete 1 major toward the single program in dual degrees, and majors are no longer possible in dual degrees from 2026.

column2: Is Software Engineering available as a major in the BE(Hons)/IT dual degree?
column3: The IT component of the BE(Hons)/IT offers majors available within the BIT program. For the specific majors currently offered in that dual degree, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au or contact UQ Admissions for the most current information.

column2: What majors can I pick for the IT side of the BBusMgt/IT dual degree?
column3: From 2026, dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program, and majors are no longer possible in dual degrees. For the current structure of the BBusMgt/IT, refer to Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: If I start BIT in 2026, can I change my major after I've already enrolled?
column3: It is generally possible to change your major, but timing and unit completion may affect your study plan and graduation timeline. Contact the School of EECS or a UQ academic advisor to discuss changing your major without losing credit.

column2: Does the Data Science major in BIT involve a lot of maths?
column3: Data Science typically involves mathematical and statistical components as part of its curriculum. For the specific course requirements and content of the Data Science major, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: What is the difference between the Information Systems major and the Information Technology major in the BIT?
column3: The specific distinctions between these majors are outlined in Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au. Generally, Information Systems tends to focus on business and organisational applications of technology, while Information Technology covers broader technical foundations. Check the official program page for exact course requirements.

column2: Can I do a Computer Science major in the BCom/IT dual degree?
column3: From 2026, majors are no longer possible in dual degrees; students may only complete 1 major toward the single program. The BCom/IT Commerce component offers majors in Accounting, Business Analytics, Business Information Systems, and Finance. Check study.uq.edu.au for the current dual degree structure.

column2: I'm interested in cybersecurity — is there a major or specialisation in that area within the BIT?
column3: For the most current list of BIT majors and any cybersecurity-related specialisations, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au, as offerings are reviewed and may be updated annually.

column2: Does UQ's BIT have a major focused on artificial intelligence or machine learning?
column3: For the current list of available BIT majors, including any AI or machine learning specialisations, refer to Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au, as the major offerings are reviewed regularly.

column2: What majors are on offer for the IT component of the BCom/IT dual degree?
column3: From 2026, majors are no longer possible in dual degrees; students in the BCom/IT may only complete 1 major toward the single program. The Commerce component offers majors in Accounting, Business Analytics, Business Information Systems, and Finance. See study.uq.edu.au for full details.

column2: If I pick a major in my BIT, does it appear on my degree certificate when I graduate?
column3: For information on how your major is recorded on your testamur or academic transcript, contact UQ Student Services or refer to the relevant program rules at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: How many units make up a major in the BIT program?
column3: For the specific unit requirements of each major within the 48-unit BIT program, refer to Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au or the EECS study plans at eecs.uq.edu.au.

column2: I'm considering the BIT/Arts dual degree. What Arts majors can I pick alongside my IT studies?
column3: The BIT/Arts dual degree allows you to choose from a wide range of Arts majors, including Psychology. From 2026, the IT side does not include an IT major; check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the full list of available Arts majors.

column2: Is the Computer Science major in the BIT accredited by any professional body?
column3: Yes. The BIT is accredited by the Australian Computer Society (ACS), which covers the program as a whole. For details on whether specific majors carry additional accreditation, check study.uq.edu.au or contact the School of EECS.

column2: Do all BIT majors follow the same study plan, or are they different semester by semester?
column3: Each major has its own suggested semester-by-semester course sequence. Study plans for all BIT majors are available from the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au and through Programs and Courses.

column2: Can I switch from the Information Technology major to the Software Engineering major after first year?
column3: Switching majors is generally possible, though it may affect your course progression and study plan. Speak with a UQ academic advisor at the School of EECS as early as possible to minimise any impact on your graduation timeline.

column2: What is the recommended study plan for the Data Science major in BIT?
column3: The semester-by-semester study plan for the Data Science major is available from the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au and through Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: I heard that from 2026 you can't do majors in dual degrees anymore. Does that mean I lose flexibility in what I study?
column3: From 2026, dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program, and majors are no longer available in dual degrees. However, you can still tailor your studies through elective choices. For advice on how to make the most of your dual degree structure, contact a UQ academic advisor.

column2: I want to study both Software Engineering and Data Science in my BIT — is that possible?
column3: The BIT is a 3-year single-major program, so you can only complete one major within the standard 48-unit structure. If you want to study across both areas, consider a dual degree or seek academic advice about elective options.

column2: What IT majors can I choose in the standalone BIT from 2026?
column3: From 2026, students in the standalone BIT may complete 1 major. Available majors include Computer Science, Data Science, Information Systems, Information Technology, and Software Engineering. Confirm current offerings at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: If I enrol in the BIT/Arts dual degree, can I still pick an IT major?
column3: No. From 2026, the BIT/Arts dual degree does not include an IT major. Dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single (Arts) component, and majors are no longer possible in the IT part of the dual.

column2: What majors are available in the Arts component of the BIT/Arts dual degree?
column3: The Arts component of the BIT/Arts dual degree offers a wide range of majors across humanities and social sciences, including Psychology. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the full list of available Arts majors.

column2: Does the BBusMgt/IT dual degree let me pick both a Business and an IT major?
column3: From 2026, dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program, so majors are no longer possible in both components. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the current major structure of the BBusMgt/IT.

column2: What majors does the Bachelor of Commerce / IT offer on the Commerce side?
column3: The Commerce component of the BCom/IT offers majors in Accounting, Business Analytics, Business Information Systems, and Finance.

column2: I'm really into cybersecurity — is there a cybersecurity major in the BIT?
column3: The BIT does not list a dedicated cybersecurity major in its current structure. For the most up-to-date major options, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au, or contact the School of EECS to ask about cybersecurity-related courses within existing majors.

column2: Can I switch my BIT major after I've already started the program?
column3: It may be possible to change your major after commencing, but this depends on your completed courses and how they count toward the new major. Contact UQ Student Services or your academic advisor to discuss your specific situation.

column2: What's the difference between the Information Technology major and the Computer Science major in the BIT?
column3: Both are available majors within the BIT, but they differ in focus — Computer Science typically emphasises computational theory and algorithms, while Information Technology covers broader applied IT concepts. For detailed course-level differences, compare the study plans at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses.

column2: Does the BE(Hons)/IT dual degree allow me to pick an IT major from 2026?
column3: From 2026, dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program, meaning majors are no longer possible in the IT component of the BE(Hons)/IT. Check study.uq.edu.au for the current structure.

column2: Which BIT major would be best if I want to work in data analytics after graduating?
column3: The Data Science major is likely most aligned with a career in data analytics. For personalised advice on choosing a major to match your career goals, contact UQ's career services or the EECS School directly.

column2: Is the Software Engineering major in the BIT the same as the Software Engineering program in the BE(Hons)?
column3: They are not the same — the Software Engineering major within the BIT is part of a 3-year IT degree, while the BE(Hons) in Software Engineering is a separate accredited engineering program. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the distinctions.

column2: Do all BIT majors have the same prerequisite high school subjects?
column3: Entry prerequisites apply at the program level rather than the major level for the BIT, so the same entry requirements apply regardless of which major you choose. Check study.uq.edu.au for the current program prerequisites.

column2: Where can I find the list of courses required for each BIT major?
column3: The course requirements for each BIT major are available through Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au and through study plans on the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au.

column2: If I pick the Information Systems major, will my degree still be ACS accredited?
column3: Yes. The BIT is accredited by the Australian Computer Society (ACS). For details on whether accreditation applies equally across all majors, confirm with the EECS School or check study.uq.edu.au.

column2: How do I decide between the Data Science major and the Information Systems major in the BIT?
column3: Data Science focuses on analysing and interpreting large datasets, while Information Systems emphasises how IT solutions support organisations and business processes. Review the study plans at eecs.uq.edu.au and seek academic advice if you are unsure which aligns best with your goals.

column2: Can I complete a major in Computer Science through the BIT/Arts dual degree?
column3: No. From 2026, the BIT/Arts dual degree does not include an IT major, and majors are no longer possible in the IT component of dual degrees. Only 1 major toward the single (Arts) program is available.

column2: Do BIT majors affect the total number of units I need to graduate?
column3: No. Regardless of which major you choose, you still need to complete 48 units in total to graduate from the 3-year BIT. The major determines which specific courses fill those units.

column2: Is there a Human-Computer Interaction or UX major available in the BIT?
column3: A standalone Human-Computer Interaction or UX major is not listed among the current BIT majors. However, relevant content is covered in courses like DECO1400 (Introduction to Web Design). Check study.uq.edu.au or contact EECS for the most current major offerings.

column2: I'm interested in both data and software — which BIT major would suit me better?
column3: Data Science focuses on analytics, machine learning, and working with large datasets, while Software Engineering emphasises building and managing large-scale software systems. Consider which career outcome appeals more and review the course lists for each major at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: If I'm doing the BIT/Arts dual degree, do I still get to pick an IT major?
column3: No. From 2026, the BIT/Arts dual degree does not include an IT major. You will complete one Arts major as part of the dual degree structure.

column2: Does the BBusMgt/IT let me pick a major from the IT side?
column3: From 2026, dual degree students may only complete one major toward the single program, and majors are no longer possible in dual degrees. Consult Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the current BBusMgt/IT structure.

column2: What majors can I choose in the BCom/IT dual degree?
column3: The Commerce component of the BCom/IT offers majors in Accounting, Business Analytics, Business Information Systems, and Finance. From 2026, only one major may be completed toward the single program in dual degrees.

column2: Is the Information Systems major more business-focused than the other BIT majors?
column3: Information Systems typically bridges IT and business, covering topics like enterprise systems, business analysis, and IT management. For the exact course list and focus areas, review the major details at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: What's the difference between the Computer Science major and the Software Engineering major in the BIT?
