column3: The specific curriculum differences between these majors are not detailed in the source documents available to me. For a course-by-course comparison, review each major's study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: If I start BIT in Semester 2, will the study plan look different compared to starting in Semester 1?
column3: Yes, the recommended course sequence in your study plan may differ depending on whether you commence in Semester 1 (23 Feb 2026) or Semester 2 (27 Jul 2026). EECS provides study plans for different commencement points — check eecs.uq.edu.au for your specific plan.

column2: Does the BIT include any courses on artificial intelligence or machine learning?
column3: Artificial intelligence and machine learning are areas covered within the BIT curriculum, particularly in certain majors such as Computer Science and Data Science. For specific course details and availability, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: What level are most of the core courses in first year BIT — are they introductory or can they be quite advanced?
column3: First-year BIT courses are generally introductory level (coded at the 1000 level), designed to build foundational skills in programming, mathematics, and IT concepts. More advanced courses at the 2000 and 3000 levels are taken in later years as you progress through the program.

column2: Is INFS3202 a course I should expect in my BIT study plan?
column3: The details of INFS3202 are not confirmed in the source documents available to me. Please check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au or your major's study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au for accurate information.

column2: Can I take a course from a different BIT major as an elective, or am I locked into only courses from my chosen major?
column3: BIT students can generally use elective units to take courses from other majors or disciplines, giving you some flexibility to broaden your studies. The exact elective rules depend on your major — consult your study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au or seek academic advice to confirm your options.

column2: I'm about to start the BIT — is there a recommended order for taking my courses each semester?
column3: Yes. The School of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science (EECS) publishes suggested semester-by-semester study plans at eecs.uq.edu.au and through UQ's Programs and Courses. Following the study plan helps ensure you meet prerequisites in the correct order.

column2: What is CSSE1001 and should I take it in my first semester?
column3: CSSE1001 is Introduction to Software Engineering, and it is typically listed as a foundational first-year course in BIT study plans. It is also a prerequisite for CSSE2002 (Programming in the Large), so completing it early is strongly recommended.

column2: Is DECO1400 compulsory for all BIT students or is it an elective?
column3: DECO1400 (Introduction to Web Design) covers web design, human-centred design, and basic scripting for dynamic content. Whether it is compulsory or elective depends on your chosen major — check your specific study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses for confirmation.

column2: What happens if I fail a prerequisite course — can I still enrol in the next course in the sequence?
column3: Generally, you cannot enrol in a course if you have not passed its prerequisite. You would need to repeat the prerequisite before progressing. Contact the EECS school or a student adviser if you are unsure about your options.

column2: Does the BIT include any mathematics courses, or is it purely computing?
column3: The BIT curriculum includes mathematics-related content relevant to computing and IT. The specific required math courses depend on your major — refer to your study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses for the exact courses.

column2: I want to specialise in cybersecurity. Is there a major or set of courses for that in the BIT?
column3: The BIT offers majors across several IT disciplines. Whether a dedicated cybersecurity major or pathway exists for your commencement year should be confirmed through Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au, as offerings can change annually.

column2: What is INFS1200 and is it relevant to BIT students?
column3: INFS1200 is Introduction to Information Systems, which covers foundational concepts in managing and using information within organisations. Its relevance to your BIT depends on your chosen major — check Programs and Courses to see if it appears in your study plan.

column2: How many elective units do I get to choose freely in the BIT?
column3: The BIT requires 48 units in total over 3 years, with a combination of compulsory core courses, major-specific courses, and electives. The number of free elective units varies by major — consult your study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au for the exact breakdown.

column2: Does CSSE2002 have any courses that depend on it as a prerequisite?
column3: CSSE2002 (Programming in the Large) is itself a prerequisite for more advanced computing courses in the BIT. The specific courses that list CSSE2002 as a prerequisite are outlined in Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: I'm interested in data science topics — are there BIT courses that cover machine learning or AI?
column3: The BIT curriculum includes areas such as data management and computing, which may include machine learning or AI-related content depending on your major. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for current course offerings aligned to your interests.

column2: What is the difference between a core course and an elective in the BIT study plan?
column3: Core courses are compulsory and must be completed by all students in a given major, while electives allow you to choose from a range of approved courses to personalise your degree. Your study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au will clearly identify which courses fall into each category.

column2: Can I take DECO1400 even if it's not listed in my major's study plan?
column3: You may be able to enrol in DECO1400 as an elective if it is not already required by your major, subject to available places and any prerequisites. Confirm this with an academic adviser or through Programs and Courses.

column2: Are BIT study plans the same for students starting in Semester 2 as for those starting in Semester 1?
column3: Study plans may differ for Semester 2 starters because some courses are only offered in specific semesters, which can affect the recommended sequence. Check the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au for a Semester 2 commencement study plan specific to your major.

column2: I've heard the BIT covers social media and mobile computing — which course is that in?
column3: Social and Mobile Computing is listed as a key area of study in the BIT, but the specific course code and title covering this content should be confirmed through Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: If I want to do the BIT Honours, do I need to plan my undergraduate courses in a particular way?
column3: Yes. To be eligible for BIT Honours you need a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale, so maintaining strong academic performance throughout your BIT is important. It is also worth reviewing Honours prerequisites early and seeking academic advice to ensure your course selections support eligibility.

column2: Does the BIT have any industry placement or internship courses built into the study plan?
column3: UQ's EAIT faculty supports industry connections through employer networks and the Enhance Your Employability program, which includes internships. Whether a formal industry placement course is embedded in your BIT study plan depends on your major — check eecs.uq.edu.au for details.

column2: What is the standard number of courses I'd take each semester in the BIT?
column3: The standard full-time load is 8 units per semester, which typically means 4 courses per semester (each worth 2 units). Over 3 years this totals 48 units required to graduate.

column2: If I transfer into the BIT from another program, will my previously completed courses count toward my study plan?
column3: You may be eligible for credit toward your BIT for previously completed courses, subject to UQ's credit assessment process. Apply for credit transfer through my.UQ or contact UQ Admissions, and consult your study plan to understand how approved credit fits into your remaining requirements.

column2: Is there a course in the BIT that covers cybersecurity topics?
column3: Yes, cybersecurity is listed as one of the key areas of study within the BIT curriculum. For specific course codes and offerings, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: I want to specialise in data science within my BIT — is there a major for that?
column3: Yes, Data Science is one of the available majors within the Bachelor of Information Technology. You can find the full list of majors and their required courses through Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Where exactly can I find the semester-by-semester course sequence for my BIT major?
column3: Study plans showing the suggested semester-by-semester course sequence are available from the School of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science (EECS) website at eecs.uq.edu.au and through Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: What does DECO2500 cover and is it relevant to BIT students?
column3: DECO2500 is Human-Computer Interaction, covering user interface design principles, usability evaluation, and interaction design methods. It is relevant to BIT students, particularly those interested in the Human-Computer Interaction major or elective areas. Check Programs and Courses for prerequisites and availability.

column2: How many units is a typical BIT course worth?
column3: Most UQ undergraduate courses are worth 2 units each. Since the BIT requires 48 units to graduate over 3 years, you would typically complete around 24 individual courses across your degree.

column2: What programming languages will I learn in the BIT?
column3: The BIT curriculum covers programming as a core area of study, with languages introduced through courses such as CSSE1001. For a detailed breakdown of languages taught in each course, check the individual course profiles on Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Is there a course specifically about networks or computer networking in the BIT?
column3: Yes, computer networks and distributed computing are listed among the key areas of study in the BIT. For specific course codes and descriptions, refer to Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: What is the prerequisite for CSSE2002?
column3: The prerequisites for CSSE2002 (Programming in the Large) are CSSE1001 or ENGG1001. You must complete one of these before enrolling in CSSE2002.

column2: Can I take DECO1400 as a first-year BIT student?
column3: DECO1400 (Introduction to Web Design) is an introductory course covering web design, human-centred design, and basic scripting, making it suitable for early study. Check its course profile on Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au to confirm any prerequisites and whether it fits your major's study plan.

column2: If I'm in the BIT/Arts dual degree from 2026, do I still complete an IT major?
column3: No. From 2026, the BIT/Arts dual degree does not include an IT major. Dual degree students may only complete one major toward the single program component, and majors are no longer possible in dual degrees.

column2: How many courses would I typically take in one semester as a full-time BIT student?
column3: A standard full-time load is 8 units per semester, and since most courses are worth 2 units each, you would typically enrol in 4 courses per semester.

column2: What is INFS3202 and when should I take it in my BIT?
column3: INFS3202 is Web Information Systems, focusing on the development of web-based information systems and related technologies. For prerequisites and the recommended semester to take it within your study plan, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Are there any compulsory first-year courses that every BIT student has to take regardless of their major?
column3: Yes, the BIT includes core courses that all students complete in addition to major-specific courses. The exact list of compulsory courses depends on your major and commencement year, so review the study plan for your major at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses.

column2: I heard CSSE1001 is quite challenging — is there any preparation I should do before starting it?
column3: CSSE1001 (Introduction to Software Engineering) is a foundational course and familiarity with basic logical thinking or prior exposure to any programming can be helpful. For any formally recommended preparation or bridging resources, check the course profile on Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: What does INFS1200 cover and when should I take it?
column3: INFS1200 is Introduction to Information Systems, covering foundational concepts in data and information management. It is typically taken in the first year of the BIT; check the EECS study plans at eecs.uq.edu.au for the recommended semester.

column2: I want to specialise in data science — which BIT major should I pick?
column3: The BIT offers a Data Science major for students interested in that area. Check the study plan for that major at eecs.uq.edu.au or through Programs and Courses to see the specific course sequence.

column2: What majors are actually available in the standalone BIT program?

column2: Does the BIT have any compulsory core courses all students must take regardless of major?

column3: DECO2500 is Human-Computer Interaction, focusing on the design and evaluation of interactive systems from a user-centred perspective. BIT students may be able to take it depending on their major and elective choices; confirm availability through Programs and Courses.

column2: If I don't have a strong maths background, will I struggle with the required courses in BIT?
column3: Some BIT courses, particularly in the Computer Science and Data Science majors, involve mathematical content. Meeting the mathematics prerequisite for entry is important, and UQ offers support services if you need assistance during your studies.

column2: Are there any project-based or capstone courses in the BIT where I work on real problems?
column3: Yes, the BIT includes a capstone or industry project component in the final year where students work on substantial real-world problems. Check the study plan for your specific major at eecs.uq.edu.au for details on the relevant course.

column2: Can I take elective courses from outside the EAIT faculty in my BIT?
column3: Yes, the BIT typically includes elective units that can be drawn from other faculties, allowing you to broaden your studies. The specific number of free electives available depends on your major; check your study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au.

column2: What is INFS3202 and what level is it pitched at?
column3: INFS3202 is Web Information Systems, a third-year course covering the design and development of web-based information systems. Its prerequisites and full course description are available through Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: I heard the BIT covers artificial intelligence — is there a specific AI course in the program?
column3: Yes, Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning are listed among the key areas of study in the BIT. Specific course codes and their place in the study plan can be found through Programs and Courses or the EECS study plans at eecs.uq.edu.au.

column2: Is CSSE2010 a required course and what does it involve?
column3: CSSE2010 is Introduction to Computer Systems, covering computer architecture and low-level programming concepts. Whether it is required depends on your chosen major; check your specific study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses.

column2: Do BIT students have to do any courses on professional ethics or IT and society?
column3: Yes, the BIT curriculum includes content on professional practice and the social and ethical dimensions of IT as part of its core areas of study. Refer to Programs and Courses for the specific course covering these topics in your major.

column2: Can I get a study plan that shows me exactly which courses to take each semester for the Software Engineering major?
column3: Yes, semester-by-semester study plans for all BIT majors, including Software Engineering, are available from the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au and through Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Is ENGG1001 an alternative way to satisfy the prerequisite for CSSE2002 if I'm in an engineering-related dual degree?

column2: What does the Social and Mobile Computing area of study actually involve in the BIT?
column3: Social and Mobile Computing is listed as a key area of study in the BIT, covering the design and development of applications for social platforms and mobile devices. For the specific course covering this area, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Is there a course in the BIT that covers cybersecurity?
column3: Yes, cybersecurity is listed as one of the key areas of study within the BIT curriculum. Specific course offerings and codes are detailed in Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Does the BIT include any project-based or capstone courses?
column3: Yes, the BIT includes capstone-style project courses in the final year where you apply skills developed throughout the program. Refer to your major's study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses for the specific course codes.

column2: I want to specialise in data science — is there a major for that in the BIT?
column3: Yes, Data Science is one of the available majors in the Bachelor of Information Technology. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the full list of required and elective courses within that major.

column3: The BIT offers majors including Computer Science, Data Science, Information Systems, Information Technology, and Software Engineering, among others. Check study.uq.edu.au for the complete and current list of available majors.

column2: If I choose the Software Engineering major in BIT, what kinds of courses will I take?
column3: The Software Engineering major focuses on areas such as programming, software design, systems architecture, and project management. For the exact course list and semester sequence, refer to the study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au.

column2: Does the BIT have any courses on artificial intelligence or machine learning?
column3: Yes, artificial intelligence and related topics are part of the BIT curriculum, particularly within relevant majors such as Computer Science and Data Science. Visit Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for specific course codes and availability.

column2: How many elective units do I get in the BIT, and can I use them for any subject at UQ?
column3: The BIT is 48 units in total, with a portion allocated to elective units after completing core and major requirements. Elective options may include courses from other faculties, but you should confirm eligibility and availability through Programs and Courses or academic advice.

column2: What is DECO2500 and is it related to DECO1400?
column3: DECO2500 is Human-Computer Interaction, which builds on design and user-centred concepts introduced in courses like DECO1400. It focuses on designing and evaluating interactive systems — check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for prerequisite details.

column2: I heard there's a course on networks in the BIT — what is it called?
column3: Computer networks is a key area of study within the BIT curriculum. The specific course code and title can be found in Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au or in your major's study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au.

column2: Can I take courses from the Business school as electives in my BIT?
column3: BIT students may be able to include courses from other faculties, including Business, as electives depending on your major's requirements and course availability. Confirm your options through Programs and Courses or seek academic advice before enrolling.

column2: What is the suggested course sequence for the first semester of a BIT student?
column3: The suggested semester-by-semester course sequence for your major is available through the School of EECS at eecs.uq.edu.au and Programs and Courses. The sequence varies depending on which major you select, so always refer to your specific major's study plan.

column2: Does the BIT include any courses on cloud computing?
column3: Cloud computing topics are covered within the BIT curriculum as part of the broader IT and systems focus. For specific course codes covering cloud computing, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Is MATH1061 required for the BIT, or is that just for Engineering students?
column3: Some BIT majors, particularly those with a stronger technical or mathematical focus such as Computer Science, may include or recommend discrete mathematics courses. Check your specific major's study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses to confirm whether MATH1061 is required or recommended for your chosen major.

column2: Do all BIT majors require the same core courses in first year?
column3: Most BIT majors share a common set of core courses in the first year, with specialisation increasing in later years. The exact core requirements for each major are outlined in the study plans at eecs.uq.edu.au and Programs and Courses.

column2: Is there a course in the BIT that teaches you how to manage IT projects?
column3: Yes, IT project management is listed as one of the key areas of study within the BIT. Specific course codes and when they appear in your study sequence depend on your major — refer to eecs.uq.edu.au for your major's study plan.

column2: What programming languages will I actually learn in the BIT program?
column3: The BIT curriculum covers programming through courses such as CSSE1001 and CSSE2002, with languages varying by course and major. For a full picture of the languages and tools taught across your major's courses, review the course profiles in Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Is there a course in the BIT that covers artificial intelligence or machine learning?
column3: Yes, the BIT curriculum includes areas such as data science and computational methods that touch on AI and machine learning concepts. For specific course codes and offerings, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: What does INFS1200 cover and is it compulsory for BIT students?
column3: INFS1200 is Introduction to Information Systems, covering the design, implementation, and use of database systems. Whether it is compulsory depends on your chosen BIT major — check the relevant study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au for your major.

column2: How is the BIT study plan structured across the three years?
column3: The BIT study plan is typically structured with foundational courses in Year 1, core and major-specific courses in Year 2, and advanced major courses alongside electives in Year 3. The recommended semester-by-semester sequence for your major is available at eecs.uq.edu.au.

column2: What is DECO2500 and would it count toward my BIT degree?
column3: DECO2500 is Human-Computer Interaction, a course focusing on designing usable and accessible interactive systems. Whether it counts toward your BIT degree depends on your major — check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for enrolment eligibility and credit details.

column2: Do I have to take any maths courses as part of the BIT?
column3: Yes, the BIT includes mathematics-related courses, particularly within majors such as Data Science and Computer Science. The specific maths courses required depend on your chosen major — refer to the study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au.

column2: What is the difference between the Computer Science major and the Software Engineering major in the BIT?
column3: The Computer Science major typically focuses on algorithms, theory, and computational problem-solving, while the Software Engineering major emphasises large-scale software design, development processes, and project management. For exact course differences, compare the study plans at eecs.uq.edu.au.

column2: Are there any group project courses built into the BIT, or is it mostly individual work?
column3: The BIT includes both individual and team-based learning across its courses — for example, DECO1400 incorporates both individual and team-based exercises. Many higher-level courses also involve collaborative projects. Check individual course profiles at study.uq.edu.au for details.

column2: What is CSSE3002 and when should I take it?
column3: CSSE3002 is The Software Process, a third-year course focusing on software development methodologies and project management practices. It is typically taken in the later stages of the BIT — consult the study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au for the recommended semester.

column2: If I'm doing the Data Science major, what kind of courses should I expect?
column3: The Data Science major typically includes courses in statistics, data management, machine learning, and data visualisation. For the exact course list and study plan, visit eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Does the BIT have elective courses, and can I use them to explore topics outside my major?
column3: Yes, the BIT includes elective units that allow you to explore topics beyond your chosen major. You can use these to broaden your studies, though you should seek academic advice to ensure your selections meet program requirements.

column2: What is INFS3202 and is it available to all BIT students?
column3: INFS3202 is Web Information Systems, a course covering the design and development of web-based information systems. Availability to BIT students may depend on your major and prerequisites — check the course profile at study.uq.edu.au for full details.

column2: I heard there's a capstone or final-year project in the BIT — is that true?
column3: Many BIT majors include a capstone or project course in the final year that integrates skills developed throughout the degree. Check the study plan for your specific major at eecs.uq.edu.au to confirm whether a capstone is required.

column2: What prerequisite do I need before taking CSSE2002?
column3: To enrol in CSSE2002 (Programming in the Large), you need to have completed either CSSE1001 or ENGG1001.

column2: Is DECO1400 only for students in the Human-Computer Interaction major, or can anyone do it?
column3: DECO1400 Introduction to Web Design is not restricted solely to HCI major students — it is a course open to BIT students more broadly, though enrolment eligibility can vary. Check the course profile at study.uq.edu.au to confirm whether it is available to your major.

column2: Are the courses in the BIT the same regardless of whether I start in Semester 1 or Semester 2?
column3: The core courses and major requirements are the same, but the semester-by-semester sequence in your study plan may differ depending on your intake semester. Check the relevant study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au for the sequence that applies to your commencement.

column2: What is ENGG1001 and could it substitute for CSSE1001 in my BIT plan?
column3: ENGG1001 is Engineering Computation, a first-year course covering programming fundamentals in an engineering context. It is accepted as an alternative prerequisite to CSSE1001 for courses such as CSSE2002, but check Programs and Courses to confirm it fits your BIT major requirements.

column2: How do I know which courses are compulsory versus elective in my BIT major?
column3: The study plan for your specific BIT major, available at eecs.uq.edu.au and through Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au, clearly outlines which courses are compulsory and which are elective. If you are unsure, contact the EECS Student Office for academic advice.

column2: Is maths compulsory in the BIT or can I skip it?
column3: Mathematics is listed as a prerequisite subject for entry into the BIT, so you need to meet that requirement before enrolling. For specific course-level maths requirements within the program, check the study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses.

column2: What courses make up the core of the BIT in first year?
column3: Core first-year BIT courses typically include CSSE1001 (Introduction to Software Engineering) and DECO1400 (Introduction to Web Design), among others depending on your major. For the full semester-by-semester sequence, refer to the study plan on the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au.

column2: I want to do the Data Science major in BIT — what kinds of courses will I study?
column3: The BIT covers key areas including social and mobile computing, and data-focused topics relevant to a Data Science major. For the exact course list and study plan for the Data Science major, check Programs and Courses or the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au.

column2: What is DECO1800 and is it related to DECO1400?
column3: DECO1800 is listed in the BIT curriculum as a design-focused course building on web and interactive media concepts. For full course details including prerequisites and its relationship to DECO1400, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: If I haven't done much coding before, will I struggle with first-year BIT courses?
column3: CSSE1001 is designed as an introductory course and does not assume prior programming experience. However, consistent effort is important, and UQ provides support services to help students who find the transition challenging.

column2: Does the BIT have any compulsory industry placement or internship courses built into the study plan?
column3: The BIT includes access to employability programs and industry connections through EECS, but whether a formal placement course is compulsory depends on your major and study plan. Check the specific major study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au for details.

column2: What does CSSE2010 cover and when would I typically take it?

column2: Are there elective courses in the BIT or is everything prescribed?

column2: I'm interested in cybersecurity — does the BIT have courses in that area?
column3: The BIT covers areas relevant to cybersecurity within its curriculum. For specific course offerings in that area, check the available majors and their study plans at Programs and Courses on study.uq.edu.au.

column2: What is INFS1200 and will I need it for BIT?
column3: INFS1200 is Introduction to Information Systems, covering database concepts and information management. Whether it is required for your BIT major depends on your chosen specialisation; check your study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses.

column2: How many courses would I normally take per semester in the BIT?
column3: A standard full-time load is 8 units per semester, which typically means 4 courses of 2 units each. Over 3 years (6 semesters) this totals the 48 units required to graduate.

column2: Is CSSE3100 or any third-year course available to second-year students if they finish prerequisites early?
column3: If you complete the required prerequisites early, you may be eligible to enrol in higher-level courses ahead of schedule. Confirm eligibility with your academic advisor and check the specific prerequisites listed in Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: I heard there are project-based courses in the BIT — is that true?
column3: Yes, the BIT includes activity-based and team-based learning components, as seen in courses like DECO1400. For a full picture of project-based courses in your major, review the study plan and individual course profiles at Programs and Courses.

column2: If I fail a prerequisite course, can I still enrol in the next course in the sequence?
column3: Generally, you must pass a prerequisite course before enrolling in the course that requires it. If you fail a prerequisite, you would need to repeat it before progressing; contact your academic advisor or the EECS faculty for guidance on your specific situation.

column2: Does the BIT study plan change depending on which major I pick, or is it the same for everyone?
column3: The study plan varies by major, with different required courses and elective choices depending on your specialisation. Access the specific study plan for your chosen major at eecs.uq.edu.au or through Programs and Courses.

column2: What is COMP3506 and is it suitable for a BIT student?
column3: COMP3506 is Algorithms and Data Structures, a course covering efficient algorithm design and data structure concepts relevant to software development. Check the prerequisites and whether it fits your BIT major's requirements in Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Can I take summer semester courses to get ahead in my BIT study plan?
column3: UQ does offer some courses in summer semester, but availability varies by course. Check the Programs and Courses page or the academic calendar at uq.edu.au to see which BIT-relevant courses are offered outside Semesters 1 and 2.

column2: What does INFS1200 cover?
column3: INFS1200 is Introduction to Information Systems — it introduces students to the concepts, management, and use of information systems in organisations. It is commonly listed as a core or recommended course in several BIT majors. Check Programs and Courses for full details.

column2: I want to specialise in data science. Which BIT major should I pick and are there specific courses I should look out for?
column3: The Data Science major within the BIT is designed for students interested in that area, covering topics such as data management, machine learning, and analytics. Refer to the study plan on eecs.uq.edu.au for the specific courses required in that major.

column2: Does the BIT have any compulsory first-year courses that all students must take regardless of major?
column3: Yes, the BIT includes core courses that all students complete in addition to their major-specific courses. The exact list of required core courses depends on your commencement year, so check your study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses for your intake.

column2: How do I know which elective courses I can choose in my BIT major?
column3: Your approved elective options are listed in your study plan and in Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au. The EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au also provides semester-by-semester study plans that indicate where electives fit. Academic advisors can assist if you are unsure.

column2: Is there a project-based or capstone course at the end of the BIT?
column3: Yes, the BIT includes a capstone project component where students apply their skills to a substantial project, typically in the final year. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the specific course code and requirements for your major.

column2: Can I take courses from other faculties as part of my BIT electives?
column3: BIT students may be able to take elective courses from other faculties depending on the rules of their program and major. You should check Programs and Courses and seek academic advice from EECS to confirm which courses count toward your degree requirements.

column2: What is MATH1061 and do BIT students have to take it?
column3: MATH1061 is Discrete Mathematics, which covers foundational mathematical concepts relevant to computing such as logic, sets, and graph theory. It is required for certain BIT majors, so check your specific major's study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au to confirm whether it applies to you.

column2: I'm interested in the Human-Computer Interaction major — what kinds of courses will I be taking?
column3: The Human-Computer Interaction major focuses on user experience design, interface design, and interactive system development. Courses like DECO1400 and DECO2500 are relevant to this pathway. Refer to Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the full course list.

column2: What is the difference between a core course and an elective in the BIT program?
column3: Core courses are compulsory and must be completed by all students in the program or major, while electives allow you to choose from an approved list to tailor your degree. The distinction for your specific major and commencement year is outlined in Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: If I'm doing the Software Engineering major, which second-year courses should I expect?
column3: The Software Engineering major typically includes courses on programming, software architecture, and systems design in second year, building on first-year foundations like CSSE1001. For the exact semester-by-semester sequence, refer to the study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au.

column2: Is STAT1301 or any statistics course part of the BIT?
column3: Statistics courses may be included in certain BIT majors, particularly Data Science. Whether STAT1301 specifically is required depends on your major and study plan, so check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au or your study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au.

column2: Can I take DECO1400 even if I'm not in the Human-Computer Interaction major?
column3: DECO1400 Introduction to Web Design may be available as an elective to students outside the Human-Computer Interaction major depending on your program rules. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au or consult an academic advisor to confirm eligibility.

column2: How many courses do I typically take per semester in the BIT?
column3: A standard full-time load is 8 units per semester, which typically equates to 4 courses of 2 units each. Over 3 years you complete 48 units in total to graduate.

column2: Is there a recommended study plan for the Computer Science major within the BIT?
column3: Yes, semester-by-semester study plans for all BIT majors, including Computer Science, are available on the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au and through Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Where exactly do I find the semester-by-semester study plan for my BIT major?
column3: Study plans are available from the School of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science (EECS) website at eecs.uq.edu.au and through Programs and Courses. They outline the recommended course sequence for each major.

column2: I want to do the Data Science major in BIT — what kinds of courses would I be taking?
column3: The BIT Data Science major covers areas such as data management, statistical analysis, machine learning, and programming. For the exact course list and study plan, visit eecs.uq.edu.au or the Programs and Courses catalogue at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Are there any courses in the BIT that involve working in teams on real projects?
column3: Yes, several BIT courses incorporate team-based and project-based learning. For example, DECO1400 includes both individual and team-based exercises as part of its activity-focused learning approach. Check individual course profiles in Programs and Courses for further details.

column2: What is CSSE2010 about?
column3: CSSE2010 is Introduction to Computer Systems, which covers low-level computing concepts including computer architecture, assembly language, and hardware-software interaction. Check the full course profile at study.uq.edu.au for prerequisites and assessment details.

column2: If I don't complete CSSE1001, can I still enrol in CSSE2002?
column3: No. The prerequisites for CSSE2002 (Programming in the Large) are CSSE1001 or ENGG1001, so you must complete one of those before enrolling. Check your study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au to ensure you take prerequisite courses in the correct sequence.

column2: Does the BIT have any courses specifically on human-computer interaction or UX design?
column3: Yes. DECO1400 Introduction to Web Design introduces human-centred design techniques, including heuristic-based interface design guidelines and web accessibility, which are core UX and HCI concepts. Additional design-focused courses may be available depending on your major; check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Is there a course in the BIT that teaches artificial intelligence or machine learning?
column3: Yes, artificial intelligence and machine learning are listed among the key areas of study in the BIT. For specific course codes and availability by semester, consult the Programs and Courses catalogue at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: What is DECO2500 and should I take it in second year?
column3: DECO2500 is Human-Computer Interaction, which focuses on user interface design and evaluation methods. For whether it fits your second-year plan, check the recommended study plan for your major at eecs.uq.edu.au.

column2: Does the BIT include any networking or communications courses?
column3: Yes, computer networks and communications are listed as part of the BIT curriculum. For the specific course codes and when to take them, refer to your major's study plan at eecs.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: What is INFS3202 and what year is it usually taken?
column3: INFS3202 is Web Information Systems, which covers advanced web-based systems and technologies. It is typically taken in later years of the BIT; check the study plan for your specific major at eecs.uq.edu.au for the recommended semester.

column2: Can I take elective courses outside of IT or computing in my BIT?
column3: Yes, the BIT includes elective units that allow you to broaden your studies outside of core IT and computing. For guidance on how many elective units are available and which courses qualify, consult Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au or seek academic advice.

column2: I'm interested in the Games major — what sorts of courses does it include?
column3: The BIT Games major covers areas such as game design, interactive systems, and computer graphics. For the full list of required courses and the recommended study plan, visit eecs.uq.edu.au or the Programs and Courses catalogue at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Does the BIT have a capstone or final-year project course?
column3: Yes, the BIT includes a capstone project course in the final year where students apply their skills to a substantial IT project. For the specific course code and requirements for your major, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: What is COMP3702 and is it relevant to the BIT?
column3: COMP3702 is Artificial Intelligence, which covers topics such as search algorithms, knowledge representation, and intelligent agent design. It is relevant to BIT students interested in AI; check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au to confirm whether it fits your major's requirements or elective options.

column2: If I fall behind in first year, can I still follow the standard study plan, or will prerequisite chains cause problems?
column3: Falling behind in first year can affect your ability to take second- and third-year courses on schedule, because many advanced BIT courses have prerequisite requirements that must be met in sequence. It is important to seek academic advice early from EECS or UQ Student Services to adjust your plan without disrupting your progress.

column2: What dual degrees can I combine with the BIT at UQ?
column3: You can combine the BIT with Arts, Business Management, Commerce, or Engineering (Honours). Each combination has its own QTAC code, entry requirements, and duration, so check study.uq.edu.au for the full list and details.

column2: How long does the Bachelor of Information Technology / Arts dual degree take to complete?
column3: The BIT/Arts dual degree takes 4 years of full-time study. Check study.uq.edu.au or Programs and Courses for the exact unit requirements for your commencement year.

column2: What is the QTAC code for the Bachelor of Information Technology / Arts?
column3: The QTAC code for the Bachelor of Information Technology / Arts dual degree is 733011. Domestic and interstate students apply through QTAC at qtac.edu.au.

column2: I'm interested in finance and IT — should I do the BCom/IT or the BBusMgt/IT?
column3: If Finance is your goal, the BCom/IT is the better fit as it offers a Finance major within the Commerce component, alongside majors in Accounting, Business Analytics, and Business Information Systems. The BBusMgt/IT has a different focus, so check study.uq.edu.au to compare the two programs before deciding.

column2: Can I do a major in both the IT and Engineering parts of the BE(Hons)/IT?
column3: No. From 2026, dual degree students may only complete one major toward the single program; majors are no longer possible in dual degrees. Check study.uq.edu.au or seek academic advice for your specific plan.

column2: What is the QTAC code for the Bachelor of Commerce / IT?
column3: The QTAC code for the Bachelor of Commerce / Information Technology is 733031. Apply through QTAC at qtac.edu.au.

column2: If I enrol in a dual degree, do I still need to meet prerequisites for IT courses?
column3: Yes. Course prerequisites apply regardless of whether you are enrolled in a single or dual degree program. You must satisfy the prerequisite requirements for each course as listed in Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Can international students apply for the BIT/Arts dual degree?
column3: Yes. International students can apply for the BIT/Arts dual degree. The indicative international tuition fee is AUD $58,056 per year for 2026, and fees are reviewed annually. Apply through UQ's international admissions process.

column2: How many units do I need to complete the BCom/IT dual degree?
column3: The exact unit count for the BCom/IT dual degree is not confirmed in my sources. Please check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the official unit requirements for your commencement year.

column2: What Arts majors can I choose in the BIT/Arts dual degree?
column3: Psychology is one confirmed major available in the BIT/Arts dual degree. For the full list of available Arts majors, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au, as options may vary by commencement year.

column2: Will doing a dual degree mean I graduate later than a standard BIT student?
column3: Yes. Dual degrees take longer than the standard 3-year BIT. For example, the BIT/Arts takes 4 years full-time. The exact duration varies by combination, so check study.uq.edu.au for the program you are considering.

column2: Can I add a Concurrent Diploma in Languages on top of my dual degree?
column3: The Concurrent Diploma in Languages can generally be studied alongside bachelor's programs at UQ. However, given the additional load of a dual degree, you should seek academic advice before applying. Domestic students can apply at study.uq.edu.au/study-options/programs/diploma-languages-1602.

column2: Are dual degree students eligible for HECS-HELP if they hold a Commonwealth Supported Place?
column3: Yes. Domestic students in Commonwealth Supported Places across all UQ undergraduate programs, including dual degrees, can access HECS-HELP as long as they meet all Commonwealth eligibility requirements.

column2: What engineering disciplines can I pair with IT in the BE(Hons)/IT?
column3: The specific engineering disciplines available within the BE(Hons)/IT are not confirmed in my sources. Please check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the full list of available engineering majors for your commencement year.

column2: If I start a dual degree but want to drop one component, can I switch to just the BIT?
column3: Program changes such as dropping one component of a dual degree may be possible, but you should contact UQ Admissions or a Student Adviser to understand the process, any academic credit implications, and how it affects your enrolment and fees.

column2: Do all BIT dual degrees have the same Semester 1, 2026 commencement date?
column3: Yes. Semester 1, 2026 commences on 23 February 2026 for all UQ undergraduate programs, including BIT dual degrees. Semester 2, 2026 commences on 27 July 2026.

column2: How long does it take to finish a BIT dual degree compared to the standalone BIT?
column3: The standalone BIT takes 3 years full-time. Dual degrees are longer due to the combined unit requirements; for example, the BE(Hons)/IT typically takes 5 years. Check the specific dual degree program page at study.uq.edu.au for the exact duration.

column2: I want to do BIT with Engineering. What is the minimum entry score I need?
column3: The minimum entry score for the Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) / IT for Semester 1, 2026 is 84. Meeting the minimum does not guarantee admission, and entry scores are reviewed annually.

column2: If I do the BIT/Arts dual degree, do I get to pick an IT major as well?
column3: No. From 2026, the BIT/Arts dual degree does not include an IT major. Dual degree students may only complete one major toward the single program, and majors are no longer possible in dual degrees.

column2: Can I do two majors if I enrol in a BIT dual degree from 2026?
column3: No. From 2026, dual degree students may only complete one major toward the single program; majors are no longer possible in dual degrees.

column2: What IT majors are available if I choose the BIT/Arts dual degree?
column3: From 2026, the BIT/Arts dual degree does not include an IT major component. You can choose an Arts major such as Psychology. For details on available Arts majors, check study.uq.edu.au.

column2: I'm interested in both finance and IT — which dual degree would suit me best?
column3: The Bachelor of Commerce / IT may suit you well, as the Commerce component offers a Finance major alongside your IT studies. The minimum entry score is 84.4 for Semester 1, 2026.

column2: Does the BCom/IT cover business analytics as a major option?
column3: Yes. The Commerce component of the BCom/IT offers majors in Accounting, Business Analytics, Business Information Systems, and Finance.

column2: Can international students apply for the BBusMgt/IT dual degree?
column3: Yes, international students can apply for the Bachelor of Business Management / Information Technology. The 2026 international tuition fee is AUD $58,056 per year, and fees are reviewed annually.

column2: If I start a BIT dual degree, can I later switch to just the standalone BIT?
column3: It may be possible to transfer between programs, but this is subject to academic approval and may affect your credit and progression. Contact UQ Admissions or your academic advisor at ask.uq.edu.au to discuss your options.

column2: Are dual degree students eligible for HECS-HELP if they are domestic students?
column3: Yes. Domestic students in Commonwealth Supported Places across all UQ undergraduate programs, including dual degrees, can access HECS-HELP as long as they meet all Commonwealth supported place eligibility requirements.

column2: Do I need to meet prerequisites for both degrees when applying for a BIT dual degree?
column3: Yes. You generally need to satisfy the entry requirements for both components of a dual degree program. Check the specific prerequisite and entry score requirements for your chosen combination at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: What CRICOS code should I use for my student visa if I'm applying for the BE(Hons)/IT?
column3: The CRICOS code for the Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) / IT is different from the standalone BIT; the BIT alone carries CRICOS code 001952K. For the correct CRICOS code for the BE(Hons)/IT, check study.uq.edu.au or contact UQ International Admissions.

column2: I'm considering the BIT/Arts dual degree — can I pick History as my Arts major?
column3: The available Arts majors within the BIT/Arts dual degree are listed on the Programs and Courses page at study.uq.edu.au. Check there to confirm whether History is among the approved options.

column2: Does completing a BIT dual degree still take me 48 units, or is it more?
column3: No. The 48-unit requirement applies to the standalone 3-year BIT. Dual degrees require additional units to satisfy both program components, so the total will be higher. Refer to the specific dual degree program page at study.uq.edu.au for the exact unit count.

column2: What's the difference between doing a BIT with a second major versus doing a BIT dual degree?
column3: From 2026, the standalone BIT is a single-major program, and dual degree students may also only complete one major toward the single program. A dual degree gives you two separate qualifications (e.g. BIT and BCom), while the standalone BIT focuses on one IT major. Consider a dual degree if you want formal recognition in two distinct disciplines.

column2: How long does a BIT dual degree take to complete?
column3: Most BIT dual degrees take longer than the standard 3-year BIT. Duration varies by combination — for example, the BE(Hons)/IT is typically 5 years. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the specific duration of the dual degree you are interested in.

column2: I want to do BIT and Engineering together. What is the minimum entry score?
column3: The minimum entry score for the Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) / IT is 84 for Semester 1, 2026. Meeting the minimum does not guarantee admission, and entry scores are reviewed annually.

column2: What is the minimum ATAR to get into the BBusMgt/IT dual degree?
column3: The minimum entry score for the Bachelor of Business Management / Information Technology is 86.4 for Semester 1, 2026. Entry scores are reviewed annually, so check study.uq.edu.au for the most current information.

column2: If I enrol in the BIT/Arts dual degree, can I complete an IT major as well?
column3: No. From 2026, the BIT/Arts dual degree does not include an IT major. Additionally, dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program, so majors are no longer possible in dual degrees.

column2: I heard you can do two majors in a dual degree — is that still the case?
column3: No. From 2026, students in dual degrees may only complete 1 major toward the single program. Majors are no longer possible in dual degrees, so you should plan your study accordingly.

column2: Can I choose an Arts major in the BIT/Arts dual degree, and which ones are available?
column3: Yes, you can choose an Arts major in the BIT/Arts dual degree — for example, Psychology is an available option. For the full list of Arts majors available within the dual degree, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: As an international student, am I eligible to apply for the BBusMgt/IT dual degree?
column3: Yes, international students can apply for the Bachelor of Business Management / Information Technology dual degree. You should ensure you meet the entry score, English proficiency, and prerequisite requirements, and apply through UQ's international admissions process.

column2: What IT majors can I study if I choose the BCom/IT dual degree?
column3: The Commerce component of the BCom/IT offers majors in Accounting, Business Analytics, Business Information Systems, and Finance. From 2026, only 1 major toward the single program is permitted in dual degrees.

column2: Does the BE(Hons)/IT dual degree have ACS accreditation?
column3: The Bachelor of Information Technology is accredited by the Australian Computer Society (ACS). For specific accreditation details relating to the BE(Hons)/IT dual degree, check study.uq.edu.au or contact UQ Admissions directly.

column2: What are the approximate domestic tuition fees if I do the BIT/Arts dual degree?
column3: Domestic tuition fees for dual degrees depend on the specific program and unit mix. For the Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) / IT, approximate annual domestic tuition is AUD $8,285 per year based on 16 units for 2026. For other dual degree fee estimates, check study.uq.edu.au as fees are reviewed annually.

column2: If I'm an international student in a BIT dual degree, how many hours can I work during semester?
column3: International students on a student visa can work up to 48 hours per fortnight during semester, as of 1 July 2023. This applies regardless of whether you are enrolled in a single or dual degree program.

column2: Should I choose the BBusMgt/IT or the BCom/IT — what's the difference?
column3: Both combine IT with a business discipline, but BCom/IT focuses on commerce areas like Accounting, Business Analytics, Business Information Systems, and Finance, while BBusMgt/IT is oriented toward broader business management. The minimum entry scores also differ — 86.4 for BBusMgt/IT and 84.4 for BCom/IT. Reviewing each program's structure at study.uq.edu.au will help you decide which aligns better with your career goals.

column2: Can I switch from the standalone BIT into a dual degree after I've already started studying?
column3: Transferring from a single BIT into a dual degree program may be possible, but you would need to meet the entry requirements for the dual degree and follow UQ's program transfer process. Contact UQ Admissions or your academic advisor for guidance specific to your situation.

column2: Do BIT dual degree students need to apply through QTAC the same way as single BIT applicants?
column3: Yes. Domestic students applying for BIT dual degrees apply through QTAC at qtac.edu.au, each dual degree has its own QTAC code — for example, the BBusMgt/IT code is 710401. Interstate students also apply through QTAC.

column2: I'm considering the BE(Hons)/IT — can international students apply and what is the CRICOS code?
column3: Yes, international students can apply for the BE(Hons)/IT dual degree. For the specific CRICOS code for this dual degree, check study.uq.edu.au or contact UQ International Admissions, as the CRICOS code differs from the standalone BIT (001952K).

column2: If I do a BIT dual degree, can I still add the Concurrent Diploma in Languages?
column3: The Concurrent Diploma in Languages can generally be studied alongside most UQ bachelor's degrees. If you are a domestic student you can apply via study.uq.edu.au, or if international, lodge through mySI-net before the due date listed in the academic calendar. Confirm eligibility for your specific dual degree combination with UQ Admissions.

column2: If I do the BIT/Arts dual degree, do I still get to pick an IT major?
column3: No. From 2026, the BIT/Arts dual degree does not include an IT major. You may complete one major toward the single-program component only; majors are no longer available in dual degrees.

column2: What is the QTAC code for the BBusMgt/IT dual degree?
column3: The QTAC code for the Bachelor of Business Management/Information Technology is 710401.

column2: What is the minimum entry score I need to get into the BE(Hons)/IT?
column3: The minimum entry score for the Bachelor of Engineering (Honours)/Information Technology for Semester 1, 2026 is 84. Entry scores are reviewed annually, so confirm the current figures at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: What majors can I choose for the Commerce side of the BCom/IT dual degree?
column3: The Commerce component of the BCom/IT offers majors in Accounting, Business Analytics, Business Information Systems, and Finance.

column2: I want to study both IT and maths — is there a dual degree for that at UQ?
column3: Yes, UQ offers a Bachelor of Information Technology/Mathematics dual degree. Check study.uq.edu.au for entry requirements, QTAC codes, and the program structure.

column2: If I'm in a dual degree, can I complete two IT majors — one for each program?
column3: No. From 2026, dual degree students may only complete one major toward the single-program component; majors are no longer possible in dual degrees.

column2: Can I apply for a BIT dual degree as an international student coming straight from high school?
column3: Yes, international students can apply for BIT dual degrees directly from high school, provided they meet the entry score, English proficiency, and prerequisite requirements for that specific dual degree. Apply through UQ's international admissions process at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Does the BIT/Arts dual degree let me pick Psychology as my Arts major?
column3: Yes, you can select Psychology as your Arts major in the Bachelor of Information Technology/Arts dual degree.

column2: How many total units would I need to complete in the BCom/IT dual degree?
column3: The total unit requirement for dual degrees differs from the standalone BIT's 48 units. For the exact unit count for the BCom/IT, refer to Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Are international students eligible to apply for the BE(Hons)/IT?
column3: Yes, international students can apply for the Bachelor of Engineering (Honours)/Information Technology dual degree. Check study.uq.edu.au for entry requirements and application details.

column2: What IT majors are offered inside the BIT component of a dual degree from 2026?
column3: From 2026, majors are no longer available in dual degrees, so there is no IT major within the BIT component of any dual degree program.

column2: Should I choose the BBusMgt/IT or the BCom/IT if I'm interested in finance?
column3: The BCom/IT offers a Finance major within the Commerce component, making it the more direct path if finance is your focus. The BBusMgt/IT is broader in business management; check both program structures at study.uq.edu.au to decide.

column2: Do domestic students in dual degrees still get a Commonwealth Supported Place?
column3: Yes. Domestic students in Commonwealth Supported Places across all UQ undergraduate programs, including dual degrees, can access HECS-HELP as long as they meet all eligibility requirements.

column2: What Arts majors are available in the BIT/Arts dual degree besides Psychology?
column3: A range of Arts majors are available in the BIT/Arts dual degree; Psychology is one confirmed option. For the full list of available Arts majors, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: If I start a dual degree but later want to drop one of the programs, can I do that?
column3: It may be possible to transfer to a single degree program, but this depends on your individual circumstances and academic progress. Contact UQ Admissions or a Student Advisor for guidance specific to your situation.

column2: Does doing a dual degree affect my eligibility to add the Concurrent Diploma in Languages?
column3: The Concurrent Diploma in Languages can generally be studied alongside most bachelor's degrees at UQ, but you should confirm compatibility with your specific dual degree by consulting study.uq.edu.au or contacting UQ directly before applying.

column2: What is the minimum entry score for the Bachelor of Business Management / IT?
column3: The minimum entry score for the BBusMgt/IT for Semester 1, 2026 is 86.4. Meeting the minimum does not guarantee admission, and entry scores are reviewed annually.

column2: What is the minimum entry score for the BE(Hons)/IT?
column3: The minimum entry score for the Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) / IT for Semester 1, 2026 is 84. Entry scores are reviewed annually, so confirm the latest figures at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: If I enrol in the BIT/Arts dual degree, do I get to choose an IT major as well?
column3: No. From 2026, the BIT/Arts dual degree does not include an IT major. Dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program, and majors are no longer possible in the dual degree component.

column2: What majors are available to me if I do the BCom/IT dual degree?
column3: In the BCom/IT, the Commerce component offers majors in Accounting, Business Analytics, Business Information Systems, and Finance. From 2026, students may only complete 1 major toward the single program.

column2: I'm interested in combining IT with maths — is that an option at UQ?
column3: Yes. UQ offers a Bachelor of Mathematics / Information Technology dual degree. For specific entry requirements, QTAC codes, and program structure, visit study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Can I still get HECS-HELP if I'm enrolled in a BIT dual degree as a domestic student?
column3: Yes. Domestic students in Commonwealth Supported Places across all UQ undergraduate programs, including dual degrees, can access HECS-HELP provided you meet all eligibility requirements.

column2: Does doing a dual degree with BIT mean I graduate with two separate degrees?
column3: Yes. Completing a BIT dual degree means you graduate with two separate qualifications — for example, a Bachelor of Information Technology and a Bachelor of Business Management — recognised as distinct awards.

column2: I want to do the BE(Hons)/IT — can I apply as an international student?
column3: Yes. International students can apply for the Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) / IT. Check study.uq.edu.au for international entry requirements and application procedures.

column2: If I'm in a BIT dual degree, how many majors can I complete?
column3: From 2026, dual degree students may only complete 1 major toward the single program; majors are no longer possible in the dual degree component itself.

column2: What Psychology-related options do I have if I choose the BIT/Arts dual degree?
column3: You can select Psychology as your major in the BIT/Arts dual degree. This allows you to combine IT skills with a formal qualification in Arts with a Psychology focus.

column2: Are the tuition fees higher for a BIT dual degree compared to the standalone BIT as an international student?
column3: Fees for dual degrees vary depending on the programs combined, and annual international tuition is reviewed each year. For specific dual degree fee information, visit study.uq.edu.au or contact UQ Admissions directly.

column2: What if I start a standalone BIT but later want to switch to a dual degree — is that possible?
column3: It may be possible to transfer into a dual degree program, but this depends on availability, entry requirements, and academic progression. You should contact UQ Admissions or a student adviser to discuss your specific situation.

column2: Does the BIT/Arts dual degree still require a mathematics prerequisite?
column3: Entry prerequisites are set per program and intake year. You should confirm the specific subject prerequisites for the BIT/Arts dual degree at study.uq.edu.au or by contacting UQ Admissions.

column2: How does the workload of a BIT dual degree compare to doing just the BIT?
column3: A dual degree requires more total units and typically adds one to two years of full-time study compared to the standalone 3-year BIT. The standard full-time load remains 8 units per semester, but the program extends to accommodate both qualifications.

column2: Is the BE(Hons)/IT accredited in the same way as the standalone BIT?
column3: The standalone BIT is accredited by the Australian Computer Society. For specific accreditation details applying to the BE(Hons)/IT dual degree, visit study.uq.edu.au or contact the EECS school directly.

column2: Should I choose the BBusMgt/IT or the BCom/IT if I'm interested in both business and technology?
column3: Both combine IT with business skills, but they differ in focus: BBusMgt/IT suits broader management interests while BCom/IT includes majors such as Accounting, Finance, and Business Analytics.
