column2: What if I miss the QTAC application deadline for the BIT? Can I still get in?
column3: Late applications may be accepted through QTAC depending on available places, but this is not guaranteed. Check qtac.edu.au for late round options and closing dates.

column2: I'm an international student. Do I apply through QTAC or directly to UQ?
column3: International students apply directly to UQ, not through QTAC. You can apply via the UQ online application portal at study.uq.edu.au. A non-refundable application fee of AUD $150 applies.

column2: Is there a specific QTAC code I need to use when applying for the BIT as a domestic student?
column3: Yes. The QTAC code for the Bachelor of Information Technology is 733001. Use this code when submitting your application through qtac.edu.au.

column2: I want to start the BIT in Semester 2. What date does that semester begin in 2026?
column3: Semester 2, 2026 commences on 27 July 2026. Check QTAC or study.uq.edu.au for relevant application deadlines for that intake.

column2: Do I need to submit any supporting documents when I apply for the BIT internationally, or just fill in the form?
column3: International applicants typically need to provide academic transcripts, proof of English proficiency, and identity documents alongside the application form. Check study.uq.edu.au for the full list of required documents for your specific situation.

column2: I finished high school overseas and I'm not sure if my results will be accepted. How does UQ assess international qualifications?
column3: UQ assesses international qualifications for equivalency to Queensland Year 12 results. Visit study.uq.edu.au or contact UQ Admissions to confirm whether your specific qualification is recognised and how it converts to an entry score.

column2: Can I use a UQ-approved agent to handle my international application instead of doing it myself?
column3: Yes. UQ works with approved agents worldwide who can assist with your application. You can find a list of approved agents at https://study.uq.edu.au/contact/find-approved-uq-agent-near-you.

column2: If I apply for the BIT and don't get in, can I apply again the following semester?
column3: Yes, you can reapply in a subsequent intake. Domestic students reapply through QTAC and international students through the UQ application portal. Ensure your entry score and any prerequisites still meet the requirements at the time of reapplication.

column2: What English proficiency do I need to apply for the BIT as an international student?
column3: International students must meet UQ's English language requirements. One accepted pathway is a grade of 65 per cent or better in English from qualifications such as the CBSE. Check study.uq.edu.au for accepted tests and minimum scores relevant to your background.

column2: I'm applying as an international student — how much money do I need to show I have for the student visa?
column3: For student visa purposes, you need to demonstrate minimum living cost evidence of AUD $29,710. This is a visa requirement set by the Australian Government, separate from UQ's own application process.

column2: Does applying early in the QTAC round improve my chances of getting into the BIT?
column3: QTAC processes applications based on your entry score rather than submission order, so applying early does not give you a scoring advantage. However, applying early ensures you meet deadlines and allows time to resolve any issues with your application.

column2: I'm a domestic student from Victoria. Do I still apply through QTAC for the UQ BIT?
column3: Yes. Interstate domestic students, including those from Victoria, apply for UQ programs through QTAC. Your Year 12 results will be converted to an equivalent entry score for assessment.

column2: What is the minimum entry score I need to have a chance at getting into the BIT for Semester 1, 2026?
column3: The minimum entry score for the BIT for Semester 1, 2026 was 81.9, with a median of 87.4 and a highest of 95.45. Meeting the minimum does not guarantee admission, and entry scores are reviewed annually.

column2: I want to apply for the BBusMgt/IT dual degree. What minimum entry score should I be aiming for?
column3: The minimum entry score for the Bachelor of Business Management / Information Technology dual degree is 86.4. Entry scores are reviewed annually, so check study.uq.edu.au for the most current information.

column2: I've submitted my international application. Is the $150 application fee refundable if I change my mind?
column3: No. The AUD $150 international application fee is non-refundable, regardless of the outcome or whether you choose to withdraw your application.

column2: What if I don't meet the maths prerequisite for BIT — are there any alternative pathways to still get in?
column3: UQ may offer bridging courses or alternative entry pathways for students who do not meet prerequisites. Contact UQ Admissions or visit study.uq.edu.au to explore options available for your specific situation.

column2: I'm applying for the BE(Hons)/IT dual degree. What entry score do I need?
column3: The minimum entry score for the Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) / Information Technology dual degree is 84. Entry scores are reviewed annually, so confirm the latest figures at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: After I get an offer for the BIT, what are the next steps to actually confirm my place?
column3: Domestic students accept their offer through QTAC, while international students follow the acceptance instructions provided in their UQ offer letter, which typically includes paying a deposit and completing enrolment steps. Check your offer documentation and study.uq.edu.au for specific deadlines and requirements.

column2: I'm an international student wanting to start BIT in Semester 2, 2026. When does that semester actually begin?
column3: Semester 2, 2026 commences on 27 July 2026. Make sure your application and any visa arrangements are in place well before that date.

column2: What documents do I typically need to submit when applying to UQ as an international student?
column3: Commonly required documents include certified academic transcripts, proof of English proficiency, a copy of your passport, and any other supporting documents specified by UQ. Check study.uq.edu.au for the full document checklist for your specific program and country.

column2: I'm a domestic student and I missed the main QTAC round. Can I still apply for BIT?
column3: QTAC runs multiple offer rounds, so you may still be able to apply after the main round closes. Check qtac.edu.au for current round deadlines and available places, as late applications may be accepted depending on remaining availability.

column2: If I apply for BIT through QTAC, can I also list a dual degree like BBusMgt/IT as a preference at the same time?
column3: Yes. QTAC allows you to list multiple program preferences in a single application, so you can include both the BIT and any dual degree options such as BBusMgt/IT. Keep in mind that each program has its own minimum entry score requirements.

column2: I want to apply directly to UQ online as an international student rather than through an agent. Is that possible?
column3: Yes. International students can apply directly through UQ's online application portal at study.uq.edu.au. If you prefer assistance, UQ also works with approved agents whose details are listed at study.uq.edu.au/contact/find-approved-uq-agent-near-you.

column2: What is the non-refundable fee I have to pay when submitting my international application to UQ?
column3: There is a non-refundable application fee of AUD $150 that applies to all international applications, regardless of how you submit them.

column2: I'm applying for BIT Honours for Semester 1. What is the application deadline if I'm a domestic student versus an international student?
column3: For Semester 1, the BIT (Honours) application deadline is 31 January for domestic students and 30 November (of the prior year) for international students. You must also have a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale to be eligible.

column2: I applied for BIT but I'm worried my score might not be high enough. Is there anything else I can do to strengthen my application?
column3: Meeting the minimum entry score of 81.9 does not guarantee admission, as places are competitive. You could explore pathway options, alternative entry schemes, or contact UQ Admissions at study.uq.edu.au for guidance on your specific situation.

column2: Does UQ accept applications from students who finished high school a few years ago, or is it only for people who just completed Year 12?
column3: UQ accepts applications from both recent school leavers and non-school leavers. If you completed Year 12 in a previous year, your results may still be considered, and you may also be assessed on other criteria such as work experience or prior study. Contact UQ Admissions via study.uq.edu.au for advice on your individual circumstances.

column2: Is the Bachelor of Information Technology at UQ accredited by any professional body?
column3: Yes. The BIT is accredited by the Australian Computer Society (ACS), which means your degree is professionally recognised across the Australian IT industry and internationally.

column2: What visa do I need to study the BIT at UQ as an international student?
column3: You will need an Australian Student Visa (subclass 500) to study a full-time degree at UQ. Check the Department of Home Affairs website and study.uq.edu.au for the most current visa requirements.

column2: How many hours per fortnight can I work in Australia on a student visa while studying the BIT?
column3: From 1 July 2023, student visa holders can work up to 48 hours per fortnight during semester. There are no work hour restrictions during scheduled course breaks.

column2: How much money do I need to show as evidence of living costs when applying for an Australian student visa?
column3: You need to demonstrate at least AUD $29,710 in living cost evidence when applying for your Australian student visa. This figure is set by the Australian Government and may be updated, so confirm the current amount with the Department of Home Affairs.

column2: Does the BIT cover artificial intelligence or machine learning topics?
column3: The BIT curriculum includes areas such as data management, computing technology, and software development, with AI and machine learning content available through relevant majors and elective courses. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for specific course listings.

column2: What's the difference between the BIT and the Bachelor of Computer Science at UQ — which one should I pick?
column3: The BIT focuses on applied IT skills including software development, networks, and human-computer interaction, while the Bachelor of Computer Science typically has a stronger theoretical and mathematical foundation. Your choice should depend on whether you prefer applied industry-ready skills (BIT) or a deeper theoretical grounding (BCS); review both programs at study.uq.edu.au to compare.

column2: If I want to do a semester abroad during my BIT, is that possible?
column3: UQ offers exchange and study abroad opportunities that BIT students may be able to access. Visit study.uq.edu.au or contact UQ's Global Experiences team for details on eligibility, partner universities, and how exchange credit applies to your program.

column2: What happens if I get caught cheating or plagiarising in a BIT course at UQ?
column3: UQ takes academic integrity seriously; breaches such as plagiarism or cheating are investigated under UQ's Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and can result in penalties ranging from a grade reduction to exclusion from the program. You can review the policy at policies.uq.edu.au.

column2: Does UQ use AI detection tools to check for academic misconduct in BIT assignments?
column3: UQ has policies and systems in place to uphold academic integrity, and assessment practices are updated regularly. For the most current information on how AI use is governed in assessments, refer to your course coordinator or check policies.uq.edu.au.

column2: Will having an ACS-accredited degree like the BIT help me get skilled migration to Australia after I graduate?
column3: ACS accreditation is relevant to skills assessments for Australian skilled migration visas, as the ACS is the assessing body for ICT occupations. However, migration outcomes depend on many factors, so consult the Department of Home Affairs or a registered migration agent for advice specific to your situation.

column2: I'm thinking about doing BIT Honours after my undergraduate degree — what GPA do I need?
column3: To be eligible for the Bachelor of Information Technology (Honours), you need a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale. You should also check additional entry requirements at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Does the ACS accreditation of the BIT mean I automatically become an ACS member when I graduate?
column3: ACS accreditation means your degree meets ACS professional standards, but membership is not automatic upon graduation — you need to apply separately to become an ACS member. Visit acs.org.au for membership information.

column2: What is the SSAF and how much will I have to pay in 2026?
column3: The Student Services and Amenities Fee (SSAF) funds non-academic services and amenities on campus. For 2026, the maximum annual SSAF is AUD $373.

column2: If I'm studying BIT on a student visa, can I keep working full-time during the semester break?
column3: Yes. Student visa holders have no work hour restrictions during scheduled course breaks, so you can work full-time during official semester breaks. During semester, the limit is 48 hours per fortnight.

column2: Does UQ's BIT teach anything about cybersecurity or is that a separate degree?
column3: Cybersecurity concepts are part of the BIT curriculum, and there may be specific courses or electives covering security topics depending on your major. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the current course list.

column2: I heard UQ has an academic integrity module I have to complete — is that true for BIT students?
column3: UQ requires students to understand and uphold academic integrity standards, and there are online resources and modules available through the library and student portal. Check your course Blackboard site or uq.edu.au/integrity for details specific to your program.

column2: Will my BIT degree from UQ be recognised if I want to work as an IT professional in the UK or Canada?
column3: UQ states that its Engineering, Computer Science, and IT qualifications are internationally recognised and graduates can work anywhere in the world. For specific country licensing or registration requirements, check with the relevant professional body in that country.

column2: What if I want to take a leave of absence during my BIT — is that allowed, and does it affect my student visa?
column3: UQ does have provisions for leave of absence, but taking a leave may affect your student visa conditions as you are required to maintain full-time enrolment. You should contact UQ Student Services and seek advice from the Department of Home Affairs or a migration adviser before making any changes.

column2: What does ACS accreditation actually mean for me as a BIT graduate?
column3: ACS accreditation means your degree meets recognised professional standards for the IT industry in Australia and internationally. It can support applications for professional membership with the ACS and may strengthen your employability with employers who value accredited qualifications.

column2: I'm trying to decide between the BIT and the Bachelor of Computer Science at UQ — what's the main difference?
column3: The BIT focuses on practical IT skills including systems, networks, software development, and areas like social and mobile computing, while the Bachelor of Computer Science tends to have a stronger theoretical and mathematical foundation. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au to compare majors, course structures, and career outcomes for both programs.

column2: Does the BIT cover anything related to artificial intelligence or machine learning?
column3: The BIT curriculum includes areas relevant to AI and computing, and specific AI or machine learning courses may be available as electives or within certain majors. Check the current course list on Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the most up-to-date options for your major and commencement year.

column2: If I'm on a student visa in Australia, how many hours am I allowed to work per week during semester?
column3: From 1 July 2023, student visa holders can work up to 48 hours per fortnight during semester. Different conditions may apply during scheduled course breaks, so check the latest visa conditions with the Department of Home Affairs.

column2: Does UQ offer exchange or study abroad opportunities for BIT students?
column3: Yes. UQ offers global study opportunities including student exchange programs. You can check available destinations and how credits may apply to your BIT program at study.uq.edu.au or through the UQ Global Experiences office.

column2: What are the consequences if I'm caught cheating or committing academic misconduct in my BIT courses?
column3: UQ takes academic integrity very seriously, and misconduct such as plagiarism or cheating can result in penalties ranging from a failing grade to exclusion from the program. All students are subject to UQ's Academic Integrity Policy, available at policies.uq.edu.au.

column2: I want to apply for the BIT (Honours) — what GPA do I need?
column3: You need a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale to be eligible for the BIT (Honours) program. Additional requirements may apply, so check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for full details.

column2: What are the application deadlines for the BIT (Honours) if I'm an international student starting in Semester 1?
column3: For international students commencing in Semester 1, the BIT (Honours) application deadline is 30 November. For Semester 2, the international deadline is 31 May.

column2: I'm a domestic student hoping to start BIT (Honours) in Semester 2 — when do I need to apply by?
column3: Domestic students applying for BIT (Honours) with a Semester 2 start must submit their application by 30 June. For a Semester 1 start, the domestic deadline is 31 January.

column2: What is the SSAF and how much will I have to pay as a BIT student in 2026?
column3: The Student Services and Amenities Fee (SSAF) funds non-academic services at UQ such as student unions, clubs, and support services. The maximum annual SSAF fee for 2026 is AUD $373.

column2: If I live off campus in Brisbane while studying BIT, roughly how much should I budget for living costs each month?
column3: Off-campus living costs in Brisbane are approximately AUD $2,026–$4,151 per month, depending on your lifestyle and accommodation choice. This is a guide only and individual expenses will vary.

column2: I heard UQ has tools to detect AI-generated content in assignments — is academic integrity around AI use something I should be aware of?
column3: Yes. UQ's academic integrity policies cover the use of AI-generated content, and the rules around its use can vary by course. Always follow the specific guidelines provided by your course coordinator and refer to UQ's Academic Integrity Policy at policies.uq.edu.au.

column2: When does Semester 1, 2026 start at UQ?
column3: Semester 1, 2026 commences on 23 February 2026. Semester 2, 2026 commences on 27 July 2026.

column2: Is there a difference in how international versus domestic BIT students apply for Honours?
column3: The eligibility requirements such as the minimum GPA of 5.0 are the same, but the application deadlines differ. International students must apply by 30 November for Semester 1 or 31 May for Semester 2, while domestic students have deadlines of 31 January and 30 June respectively.

column2: I'm concerned about accidentally plagiarising in my assignments — where can I find UQ's rules on this?
column3: UQ's academic integrity guidelines, including rules on plagiarism and proper referencing, are available at policies.uq.edu.au. Many BIT courses also provide specific guidance during orientation, and UQ offers academic skills support to help you avoid unintentional misconduct.

column2: Can BIT students participate in industry internships as part of their degree?
column3: Yes. UQ supports IT students in accessing internship opportunities through the Enhance Your Employability program and the EAIT faculty's industry connections. Check study.uq.edu.au and the EECS website at eecs.uq.edu.au for specific internship course options and industry programs relevant to your major.

column2: Is the BIT accredited by any professional body in Australia?
column3: Yes. The Bachelor of Information Technology at UQ is accredited by the Australian Computer Society (ACS), which is recognised as the professional body for the ICT sector in Australia.

column2: Does ACS accreditation mean anything for my career after graduating from BIT?
column3: Yes. ACS accreditation means your qualification is recognised by the Australian Computer Society, supporting professional membership and demonstrating that your degree meets industry-recognised standards. UQ also states that its IT qualifications are internationally recognised, so graduates can work anywhere in the world.

column2: I'm really interested in AI — does the BIT cover artificial intelligence topics?
column3: Yes. Artificial intelligence is listed as one of the key areas of study in the BIT curriculum. For specifics on which courses cover AI, check the Programs and Courses directory at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: What's the difference between the BIT and the Bachelor of Computer Science at UQ? Which one should I pick?
column3: The BIT focuses on the application of technology to solve business and industry problems, covering areas like software development, networks, and social and mobile computing. The Bachelor of Computer Science has a stronger emphasis on theoretical foundations and computational thinking. The best choice depends on whether you prefer applied IT practice or deeper computer science theory — check both programs at study.uq.edu.au for full course details.

column2: How many hours per week can I work in Australia on a student visa while studying BIT?
column3: From 1 July 2023, student visa holders may work up to 48 hours per fortnight during semester. Always check the latest Department of Home Affairs guidance, as visa conditions can change.

column2: How much money do I need to show I have for a student visa to study BIT at UQ?
column3: You need to demonstrate a minimum of AUD $29,710 to meet the living cost evidence requirement for a student visa. This is a Department of Home Affairs requirement and may change, so confirm current figures at homeaffairs.gov.au.

column2: Can I do a student exchange program while studying BIT at UQ?
column3: UQ offers exchange and study abroad opportunities that some BIT students can access. For details on eligible programs, partner universities, and how exchange units count toward your degree, visit study.uq.edu.au or contact UQ's Global Experiences team.

column2: If I go on exchange during my BIT, will those units count toward my 48-unit graduation requirement?
column3: Exchange units may count toward your BIT degree, but this depends on the courses approved for credit transfer. You should seek academic advice from EECS and contact UQ's Global Experiences team before committing to confirm how units will be counted.

column2: When is the application deadline for BIT Honours if I'm an international student wanting to start in Semester 2?
column3: The application deadline for international students starting BIT (Honours) in Semester 2 is 31 May. For Semester 1, the international deadline is 30 November.

column2: I'm a domestic student hoping to start BIT Honours in Semester 1 — when do I need to apply by?
column3: Domestic students applying for BIT (Honours) with a Semester 1 start must apply by 31 January. For Semester 2, the domestic deadline is 30 June.

column2: What is academic integrity and does UQ take it seriously for IT students?
column3: Academic integrity means submitting your own original work and properly acknowledging sources. UQ has strict academic integrity policies that apply to all students, including those in BIT. Violations can result in serious penalties — refer to policies.uq.edu.au for full details.

column2: Can I use AI tools like ChatGPT to help with my BIT assignments?
column3: The use of AI tools in assessments is governed by UQ's academic integrity policies and individual course rules set by each course coordinator. You must check the specific guidelines for each course, as permitted use varies. Refer to policies.uq.edu.au and your course profiles for guidance.

column2: Does studying BIT at UQ lead to any pathways into cybersecurity roles?
column3: The BIT covers areas relevant to cybersecurity within its curriculum. For specific courses and majors aligned with cybersecurity careers, check the BIT program details and available majors at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: If I start my BIT in Semester 2, 2026, when does that semester begin?
column3: Semester 2, 2026 commences on 27 July 2026. Check the UQ academic calendar for all associated key dates.

column2: Is there a Student Services and Amenities Fee at UQ, and how much is it?
column3: Yes. The Student Services and Amenities Fee (SSAF) applies at UQ, with a maximum annual fee of AUD $373 for 2026. The amount may vary based on your study load.

column2: How much should I budget per month for living costs if I'm an international student living off-campus while doing BIT?
column3: Off-campus living costs are approximately AUD $2,026 to $4,151 per month. If you live in an on-campus residential college with meals included, expect approximately AUD $2,635 to $4,168 per month.

column2: Is the BIT (Honours) available to start in Semester 2, or only Semester 1?
column3: The BIT (Honours) is available for both Semester 1 and Semester 2 intakes.

column2: I want to report academic misconduct I witnessed in my BIT course — who do I contact at UQ?
column3: Academic integrity concerns should be reported through UQ's official channels as outlined in UQ's Academic Integrity Policy at policies.uq.edu.au. You can also contact your course coordinator or the School of EECS directly for guidance.

column2: I'm on a student visa — how many hours can I work per week while studying the BIT?
column3: From 1 July 2023, student visa holders can work up to 48 hours per fortnight during semester. Work restrictions may differ during official semester breaks, so check the latest Department of Home Affairs guidelines for current conditions.

column2: How much money do I need to show I have for a student visa to study the BIT in Australia?
column3: You need to demonstrate at least AUD $29,710 in living costs as part of your student visa evidence requirements. This is in addition to evidence of tuition fees and other costs required by the Department of Home Affairs.

column2: Can I do a student exchange or study abroad during my BIT degree?
column3: UQ offers global study opportunities, including student exchange programs, as part of its employability initiatives. You should consult the UQ Global Experiences office and check study.uq.edu.au for eligible programs and how exchange units can count toward your BIT.

column2: What is academic integrity and how serious is it at UQ for BIT students?
column3: Academic integrity means submitting your own original work and properly acknowledging others' contributions. UQ takes breaches — such as plagiarism or contract cheating — very seriously, and penalties can include course failure or exclusion from the university. All students must follow UQ's Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy available at policies.uq.edu.au.

column2: If I get caught using AI to write my BIT assignment without permission, what could happen?
column3: Submitting AI-generated content without authorisation may be treated as academic misconduct under UQ's Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy. Penalties can range from a zero grade on the assessment to exclusion from UQ depending on the severity. Always check your course profile for each course's specific rules on AI tool use.

column2: Does UQ's BIT have any special focus on cybersecurity as a major or specialisation?
column3: Yes. Cybersecurity is one of the available majors within the BIT program. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the full list of majors and their required courses.

column2: I want to apply for the BIT Honours — what GPA do I need and when are the deadlines?
column3: You need a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale to be eligible for the BIT (Honours). Application deadlines are: Semester 1 — 30 November (international) and 31 January (domestic); Semester 2 — 31 May (international) and 30 June (domestic).

column2: Is the ACS accreditation for the BIT recognised outside Australia — like if I want to work in the UK or Singapore?
column3: Yes. UQ's EAIT faculty states that qualifications are internationally recognised and graduates can work anywhere in the world. ACS also has mutual recognition agreements with professional bodies in several countries, which can support your professional standing overseas.

column2: What's the living cost I should budget for if I'm an international student studying BIT on campus at UQ?
column3: Off-campus living costs are estimated at AUD $2,026–$4,151 per month, while on-campus residential colleges cost approximately AUD $2,635–$4,168 per month including meals. These are indicative figures; actual costs will depend on your lifestyle and accommodation choices.

column2: Can I apply for a student visa before I've received my UQ offer for the BIT?
column3: No. You must hold a valid Confirmation of Enrolment (CoE) issued by UQ before you can apply for an Australian student visa. Apply for your UQ place first, then use the CoE to lodge your visa application with the Department of Home Affairs.

column2: Does completing the BIT make me eligible to apply for ACS membership after I graduate?
column3: Yes. Because the BIT is accredited by the Australian Computer Society, graduates are eligible to apply for professional ACS membership. The level of membership available will depend on your academic results and any relevant work experience.

column2: I'm worried about AI tools like ChatGPT — does UQ have a clear policy on using them in BIT courses?
column3: UQ has an academic integrity policy that covers the use of AI tools, but permitted use varies by course. Each course profile will specify whether and how AI tools may be used for assessments, so you must read course profiles carefully and ask your course coordinator if you are unsure.

column2: Is a BIT or a Bachelor of Computer Science better if I want to work in AI after graduating?
column3: Both programs can lead to AI-related careers; the BIT offers majors in areas like data science and intelligent systems with a practical focus, while the Bachelor of Computer Science provides deeper theoretical grounding. For the strongest AI preparation, review the specific course offerings in each program at study.uq.edu.au and consider your preferred balance of theory and practice.

column2: When does Semester 1, 2026 actually start at UQ for BIT students?
column3: Semester 1, 2026 commences on 23 February 2026. Consult the UQ Academic Calendar at uq.edu.au for key dates including orientation, census date, and exam periods.

column2: If I want to take a semester abroad during my BIT, will those units count toward my 48-unit requirement?
column3: It may be possible for approved exchange units to count toward your BIT's 48-unit graduation requirement, but this depends on academic approval and program rules. Contact UQ Global Experiences and your academic advisor to confirm credit transfer arrangements before committing to an exchange.

column2: Does UQ BIT teach anything about ethical issues in AI or tech, or is it all just coding?
column3: The BIT curriculum extends beyond coding and includes topics such as human-centred design, web accessibility, and social and mobile computing, which incorporate ethical and societal dimensions of technology. For specific ethics-focused courses, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: How many hours per week can I work on a student visa while studying the BIT?
column3: From 1 July 2023, student visa holders may work up to 48 hours per fortnight during semester. Always verify current conditions with the Department of Home Affairs, as these rules can change.

column2: How much money do I need to show as evidence of living costs when applying for a student visa?
column3: You need to demonstrate a minimum of AUD $29,710 to satisfy the living cost evidence requirement for an Australian Student Visa (subclass 500).

column2: How does the BIT compare to the Bachelor of Computer Science at UQ — which one should I pick?
column3: The BIT focuses on practical IT skills, software development, and industry-ready applications, while the Bachelor of Computer Science has a stronger theoretical and mathematical foundation. Your choice depends on whether you prefer applied IT practice or a more theoretical computing focus; consult study.uq.edu.au for detailed program comparisons.

column2: Can I go on a student exchange during my BIT at UQ?
column3: UQ offers study abroad and exchange programs that allow eligible students to spend time at a partner university overseas. Check the UQ Global Experiences website for partner institutions, eligibility requirements, and how exchange units count toward your BIT.

column2: What is academic integrity and how seriously does UQ take it in IT courses?
column3: Academic integrity means submitting only your own original work and properly acknowledging sources. UQ takes academic misconduct very seriously across all programs including the BIT, and penalties can include failing a course or exclusion; refer to policies.uq.edu.au for the full Student Academic Integrity Policy.

column2: If I use AI tools like ChatGPT to help write my BIT assignments, is that allowed?
column3: UQ's academic integrity policies govern the use of AI tools in assessments, and rules vary by course and assessment task. You must follow the specific instructions given by your course coordinator; using AI without permission may constitute academic misconduct.

column2: Will my ACS accreditation from the BIT be recognised if I want to work overseas after graduating?
column3: UQ states that its IT qualifications are internationally recognised and graduates can work anywhere in the world. ACS also has international agreements with professional bodies in many countries, which can support recognition abroad.

column2: What is the minimum GPA I need to apply for the BIT Honours program?
column3: You need a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale to be eligible to apply for the Bachelor of Information Technology (Honours).

column2: I'm an international student — when is the deadline to apply for BIT Honours starting in Semester 1?
column3: The application deadline for international students commencing BIT (Honours) in Semester 1 is 30 November. For Semester 2, the international deadline is 31 May.

column2: What is the SSAF and how much will it cost me in 2026?
column3: The Student Services and Amenities Fee (SSAF) is a government-mandated fee for student services. The maximum annual SSAF at UQ for 2026 is AUD $373.

column2: If I live on campus at a UQ residential college, roughly how much should I budget per month?
column3: On-campus residential college costs range from approximately AUD $2,635 to $4,168 per month for 2026, with meals included. Costs vary by college and room type.

column2: What if I prefer to live off campus — what are the monthly living cost estimates?
column3: If you live off campus, you should budget approximately AUD $2,026 to $4,151 per month to cover rent, food, transport, and other living expenses based on 2026 estimates.

column2: Does UQ have any pathway programs or foundation courses that can lead into the BIT for international students who don't meet direct entry requirements?
column3: UQ does have pathway options for students who do not meet direct entry requirements. For details on foundation and pathway programs accepted for BIT entry, visit study.uq.edu.au or contact UQ Admissions directly.

column2: When does Semester 2, 2026 start at UQ?
column3: Semester 2, 2026 commences on 27 July 2026. Check the UQ academic calendar for key dates including census and exam periods.

column2: I'm worried about plagiarism detection in coding assignments — does UQ check IT assignments for copying?
column3: Yes. UQ uses academic integrity checks including plagiarism and code similarity detection tools for IT assessments. Submitting another student's code or code from the internet without authorisation can constitute academic misconduct under UQ's policies.

column2: Can I defer my BIT offer and start in a later semester if something comes up?
column3: Deferral policies depend on your admission category and the specific intake. You should contact UQ Admissions or check study.uq.edu.au for deferral eligibility and deadlines relevant to your offer.

column2: Is the BIT at UQ recognised for skilled migration purposes in Australia?
column3: ACS-accredited qualifications like the UQ BIT are generally assessed by the Australian Computer Society for skilled migration purposes, but migration outcomes depend on Australian Government immigration rules. Consult the Department of Home Affairs or a registered migration agent for advice specific to your situation.

column2: What's the difference between the BIT and the Bachelor of Computer Science at UQ?
column3: The BIT focuses on applied IT skills including software development, networks, data management, social and mobile computing, and industry practice, while the Bachelor of Computer Science (BCompSci) has a stronger emphasis on theoretical foundations and advanced computing. Both are ACS-accredited; your choice should reflect whether you prefer applied or theoretical study.

column2: If I'm on a student visa, how many hours can I work per week during semester?
column3: From 1 July 2023, student visa holders can work up to 48 hours per fortnight during semester. There are no work restrictions during scheduled course breaks.

column2: How much money do I need to show I have to get a student visa for the BIT?
column3: To satisfy the student visa living cost requirement, you need to demonstrate a minimum of AUD $29,710 in available funds. This is in addition to evidence of tuition fee payment.

column2: Can I do a student exchange during my BIT?
column3: UQ offers exchange programs that allow students to study abroad at a partner university. Check study.uq.edu.au and the UQ Global Experiences office for eligible programs, partner institutions, and how exchange units may count toward your BIT.

column2: I heard academic integrity is taken really seriously at UQ. What happens if I'm caught cheating?
column3: UQ has a strict Student Integrity and Misconduct policy. Breaches such as plagiarism or contract cheating can result in a range of penalties including a failing grade, suspension, or exclusion. Full details are at policies.uq.edu.au.

column2: Does UQ use any AI detection tools to check for academic misconduct in BIT assignments?
column3: UQ applies its academic integrity policies to all submitted work. For specific details on assessment tools and AI use policies in BIT courses, refer to the course profiles on Programs and Courses or contact the relevant course coordinator.

column2: Is there an AI or data science major available in the BIT?
column3: For the current list of available BIT majors and whether an AI or data science specialisation is offered, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au, as offerings can be updated annually.

column2: Can I study the BIT and then apply to do Honours afterwards?
column3: Yes. After completing the BIT, eligible students can apply for the Bachelor of Information Technology (Honours). You need a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale to be considered.

column2: When is the application deadline for BIT Honours if I want to start in Semester 1 and I'm an international student?
column3: International students applying for BIT Honours commencing Semester 1 must apply by 30 November of the preceding year. Domestic students have until 31 January.

column2: What if I want to start BIT Honours in Semester 2 — when do I need to apply?
column3: For a Semester 2 start, international students must apply by 31 May and domestic students by 30 June.

column2: I'm comparing the BIT with the BCompSci — which one is better for getting a job in software development?
column3: Both degrees can lead to software development careers and are ACS-accredited. The BIT has a strong applied focus including industry practice, while the BCompSci emphasises theoretical depth. Your best fit depends on your preferred learning style; check both program structures at study.uq.edu.au and seek academic advice.

column2: Does completing a BIT at UQ allow me to apply for skilled migration to Australia after graduation?
column3: A UQ BIT qualification may support a skilled migration application, but visa eligibility depends on many factors assessed by the Australian Department of Home Affairs. Contact a registered migration agent or the UQ Student Union for migration support.

column2: Is there anything at UQ that can help me if I have a visa or immigration problem while studying?
column3: Yes. The UQ Student Union (UQU) can assist with legal or migration support. You can also contact UQ's international student support services for referrals.

column2: Will my BIT degree from UQ be recognised if I want to work in the US or UK after graduating?
column3: Yes. UQ's Engineering, Computer Science and IT faculty states that its qualifications are internationally recognised, allowing graduates to work anywhere in the world. ACS accreditation also supports international professional recognition.

column2: Are there any integrity rules about using AI tools like ChatGPT in BIT assessments?
column3: UQ's academic integrity policies cover the use of AI tools in assessments, and rules vary by course and assessment task. Always check the specific course profile and instructions from your course coordinator, as unauthorised AI use may constitute academic misconduct.

column2: Can I go on exchange overseas and have those courses count toward my BIT?
column3: It may be possible for approved exchange courses to count toward your BIT elective or program requirements, but this requires prior approval from UQ. Contact the UQ Global Experiences office and your academic advisor before committing to an exchange.

column2: Does the BIT prepare me for ACS professional membership after I graduate?
column3: Yes. Because the BIT is ACS-accredited, graduates are well positioned to apply for ACS professional membership, which can enhance your career prospects and professional standing in the Australian IT industry.

column2: How many hours per fortnight can I work in Australia while studying on a student visa?
column3: From 1 July 2023, student visa holders can work up to 48 hours per fortnight during semester. Different conditions may apply during scheduled course breaks, so confirm current rules with the Department of Home Affairs.

column2: How much money do I need to show for living costs when applying for an Australian student visa?
column3: You will need to demonstrate at least AUD $29,710 as evidence of minimum living costs when applying for a student visa. This is a government requirement, not a UQ requirement.

column2: Does UQ offer exchange or study abroad programs for BIT students?
column3: Yes. UQ has exchange and study abroad partnerships with universities around the world. BIT students can explore these opportunities through the UQ Global Experiences office at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Does the BIT at UQ cover anything related to artificial intelligence or machine learning?
column3: Yes. Artificial intelligence and data science are listed as key areas of study within the BIT curriculum, and relevant courses are available depending on your chosen major. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for specific course offerings.

column2: If I get caught cheating in a BIT course, what happens?
column3: UQ takes academic integrity very seriously. Breaches such as plagiarism or cheating can result in penalties ranging from a grade reduction to exclusion from the program. Full details are outlined in UQ's Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy at policies.uq.edu.au.

column2: Can I use AI tools like ChatGPT for my BIT assignments?
column3: UQ has an academic integrity policy that governs the use of AI tools in assessment. Whether AI assistance is permitted depends on the specific course and assessment guidelines set by your course coordinator. Always check your course's assessment instructions and UQ's academic integrity resources at integrity.uq.edu.au.

column2: Does the ACS accreditation of the BIT mean I can become a professional member of ACS after graduating?
column3: Graduating from an ACS-accredited program like the BIT can support your pathway to ACS membership, though membership requirements depend on your experience and the membership category you apply for. Check acs.org.au for full membership criteria.

column2: I'm on a student visa — am I allowed to work full-time during the semester break?
column3: Work restrictions may differ during scheduled course breaks compared to during semester. You should confirm the current conditions directly with the Department of Home Affairs, as UQ cannot provide visa advice.

column2: What if I want to do a semester abroad — will it delay my BIT graduation?
column3: Not necessarily. If courses completed on exchange are approved for credit toward your BIT, your graduation timeline may not be affected. Contact the UQ Global Experiences office and your academic advisor before committing to an exchange to plan your credit transfer.

column2: Is the BIT recognised by employers outside of Australia?
column3: Yes. UQ's qualifications are internationally recognised, and the ACS accreditation of the BIT further supports its credibility with employers globally.

column2: What minimum GPA do I need to apply for the Bachelor of Information Technology (Honours)?
column3: You need a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale to be eligible to apply for the BIT (Honours).

column2: When is the application deadline for the BIT Honours if I want to start in Semester 1 and I'm an international student?
column3: The application deadline for international students commencing BIT (Honours) in Semester 1 is 30 November. Domestic students have until 31 January.

column2: What does the BCS at UQ offer that the BIT doesn't, in terms of majors?
column3: The specific majors available in the BCS differ from those in the BIT; for example, the BCS may offer majors more focused on software engineering or theoretical computer science. Review both programs at study.uq.edu.au to compare available majors and course structures.

column2: If I'm studying BIT and I want to do an internship, does UQ help with that?
column3: Yes. UQ's Enhance Your Employability program includes internship opportunities, and the EAIT faculty has industry connections through research partnerships and employer networks relevant to IT careers.

column2: Does studying BIT at UQ give me any pathway into cybersecurity roles?
column3: Yes. Cybersecurity is listed as one of the key areas of study in the BIT, and relevant courses are available within the program. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for specific cybersecurity course options.

column2: What happens to my student visa if I take a leave of absence from my BIT program?
column3: Taking a leave of absence may affect your student visa conditions, as your visa is linked to your enrolment status. You should contact UQ's Student Services and consult the Department of Home Affairs before making any changes to your enrolment.

column2: Is there a specific application deadline for BIT Honours students starting in Semester 2 if I'm a domestic student?
column3: Yes. Domestic students applying for BIT (Honours) with a Semester 2 commencement must apply by 30 June. International students have a deadline of 31 May for the same intake.

column2: What's the difference between the BIT and the Bachelor of Computer Science at UQ? I'm not sure which one to pick.
column3: Both degrees cover computing fundamentals, but the BIT focuses on applying technology to solve business and organisational problems, while the Bachelor of Computer Science tends to have a stronger theoretical and engineering focus. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au to compare majors and course lists before deciding.

column2: Does the BIT cover any artificial intelligence topics or is that only in Computer Science?
column3: AI-related content is available within the BIT curriculum; for example, data science and computing technology are listed areas of study. For specific AI-focused courses, check the BIT major details and elective options at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: I'm on a student visa — how many hours per week can I actually work while studying at UQ?
column3: From 1 July 2023, student visa holders can work up to 48 hours per fortnight during semester. There are no restrictions on working hours during scheduled course breaks.

column2: How much money do I need to prove I have when applying for an Australian student visa to study the BIT?
column3: You need to demonstrate a minimum of AUD $29,710 in living costs as evidence when applying for your Australian student visa. This is in addition to evidence of your tuition fees.

column2: Does UQ offer any student exchange or study abroad programs for BIT students?
column3: Yes. UQ offers global study opportunities as part of its Enhance Your Employability program, which includes exchange and study abroad options. Visit study.uq.edu.au for details on available programs and how to apply.

column2: What happens if I'm caught cheating or plagiarising in a BIT course at UQ?
column3: UQ takes academic integrity very seriously. Breaches such as plagiarism or cheating are handled under UQ's Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy, and penalties can range from a grade of zero for the assessment to exclusion from the program. Always cite your sources and submit original work.

column2: If I finish my BIT and want to do Honours, what GPA do I need?
column3: To be eligible for the Bachelor of Information Technology (Honours), you need a minimum GPA of 5.0 on UQ's 7-point scale. You should also check any additional admission requirements at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: When is the application deadline for BIT Honours if I'm an international student wanting to start in Semester 1?
column3: International students applying for BIT Honours with a Semester 1 start must apply by 30 November. For a Semester 2 start, the international deadline is 31 May.

column2: I'm a domestic student and I want to start BIT Honours in Semester 2 — what's the deadline to apply?
column3: Domestic students applying for BIT Honours for a Semester 2 commencement must submit their application by 30 June. For Semester 1, the domestic deadline is 31 January.

column2: Is there a student service fee on top of my tuition at UQ, and how much is it?
column3: Yes. The Student Services and Amenities Fee (SSAF) is charged separately from tuition. For 2026, the maximum annual SSAF is AUD $373.

column2: What are the living costs I should expect if I'm an international student living off-campus near UQ?
column3: If you live off-campus, you should budget approximately AUD $2,026 to $4,151 per month for living costs. On-campus residential colleges cost approximately AUD $2,635 to $4,168 per month and typically include meals.

column2: Does the BCS (Bachelor of Computer Science) also have ACS accreditation, or is it just the BIT?
column3: The validated information confirms that the BIT is accredited by the Australian Computer Society. For details on ACS accreditation for other UQ programs such as the BCS, please check study.uq.edu.au.

column2: I've heard UQ has an academic integrity module — is it compulsory for BIT students?
column3: UQ requires all students to complete academic integrity training as part of their enrolment obligations. For specific requirements applicable to BIT students, check with UQ's Student Services or your faculty at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Can I do an internship as part of my BIT degree at UQ?
column3: Yes. Internships are included as part of UQ's Enhance Your Employability program, and the EAIT faculty has industry connections that support placement opportunities for IT students. Check study.uq.edu.au for details on how to access these opportunities.

column2: If I'm studying the BIT on a student visa and I fail a semester, could it affect my visa?
column3: Yes. Student visa holders must maintain satisfactory academic progress as a condition of their visa. Failing to meet your provider's requirements could result in a report to the Department of Home Affairs. Contact UQ's Student Services or an immigration adviser if you are at risk of failing.

column2: Does the BIT have any specific focus on machine learning or data analytics, or would I need to do Computer Science for that?
column3: The BIT includes data science as a listed area of study, so there is relevant content available. For the most detailed breakdown of which specific courses cover machine learning or analytics within the BIT, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: How is the BIT different from doing an IT major inside a Bachelor of Business Management? Which is better for someone who wants to work in tech?
column3: The BIT is a dedicated technology-focused degree accredited by the ACS, offering deeper technical specialisation, while the BBusMgt with a Business Information Systems component balances management and IT. If your primary goal is a technical IT career, the BIT provides stronger specialist grounding; for a business-technology hybrid role, the BBusMgt pathway may suit better. Visit study.uq.edu.au to compare course structures.

column2: Does UQ's BIT cover anything related to artificial intelligence or machine learning?
column3: Yes. Data Science is one of the available majors in the BIT, which includes content relevant to AI and machine learning. Check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au for the specific courses available in your chosen major.

column2: I'm on a student visa — how many hours am I allowed to work while studying the BIT?
column3: From 1 July 2023, student visa holders can work up to 48 hours per fortnight during semester. There are no restrictions on work hours during scheduled course breaks.

column2: How much money do I need to show as evidence of living costs for my student visa application?
column3: You must demonstrate a minimum of AUD $29,710 to meet the student visa living cost evidence requirement. This is a Department of Home Affairs requirement, not a UQ figure, so check immi.homeaffairs.gov.au for current details.

column2: I'm an international student hoping to apply for BIT Honours starting Semester 1 — when is the deadline?
column3: The application deadline for international students commencing BIT Honours in Semester 1 is 30 November. Domestic students have until 31 January for the same intake.

column2: If I want to start BIT Honours in Semester 2, when do I need to apply by as a domestic student?
column3: Domestic students applying for BIT Honours with a Semester 2 start must submit their application by 30 June. International students have an earlier deadline of 31 May.

column2: What is academic integrity and does UQ take it seriously for IT courses?
column3: Academic integrity means submitting your own original work and properly acknowledging sources; breaches include plagiarism and contract cheating. UQ takes academic integrity very seriously across all programs, including BIT, and breaches can result in significant penalties. Refer to policies.uq.edu.au for the full Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy.

column2: How does ACS accreditation actually help me after I graduate from the BIT?
column3: ACS accreditation means your BIT degree meets recognised professional standards, which can support your eligibility for ACS membership and may assist with skilled migration visa pathways and employer recognition in Australia and overseas.

column2: I'm thinking about living off campus while doing my BIT — roughly how much should I budget per month?
column3: Off-campus living costs are estimated at AUD $2,026–$4,151 per month, depending on your lifestyle and accommodation choice. These figures are indicative; actual costs will vary.

column2: How much does it cost to live in a UQ residential college, and does that include meals?
column3: On-campus residential colleges cost approximately AUD $2,635–$4,168 per month, and meals are included in that cost.

column2: Should I choose the BIT or the Bachelor of Computer Science if I'm more interested in building software for businesses?
column3: The BIT is generally more aligned with applying technology in business and organisational contexts, while the Bachelor of Computer Science is more theory and engineering focused. If building software for business environments is your goal, the BIT — particularly the Software Information Systems major — may suit you better, but check both program structures at study.uq.edu.au to decide.

column2: What semester does UQ's Semester 1 2026 actually start?
column3: Semester 1, 2026 commences on 23 February 2026. Semester 2, 2026 commences on 27 July 2026.

column2: Is the BIT degree from UQ recognised if I want to work overseas after graduating?
column3: Yes. UQ's Engineering, Computer Science and IT faculty states that its qualifications are internationally recognised and graduates can work anywhere in the world. ACS accreditation further supports this recognition.

column2: I've heard about internship opportunities — does the BIT program help students get work experience?
column3: Yes. UQ provides career and industry engagement support through the Enhance Your Employability program, which includes internships, industry events, and employer networks through the EAIT faculty. Check study.uq.edu.au for current opportunities available to BIT students.

column2: What happens to my student visa if I decide to take a leave of absence from the BIT?
column3: Taking a leave of absence may affect your student visa conditions, as your visa is tied to your enrolment in a registered full-time program. You should contact UQ's International Student Services and the Department of Home Affairs before making any changes to your enrolment.

column2: I'm an international student on a student visa — how many hours can I work per week during semester?
column3: From 1 July 2023, student visa holders can work up to 48 hours per fortnight during semester. There are no work hour restrictions during scheduled course breaks.

column2: What is the minimum amount of money I need to show for a student visa to study the BIT at UQ?
column3: You need to demonstrate a minimum of AUD $29,710 in living costs as part of your student visa application. This is a Department of Home Affairs requirement, so also check immi.homeaffairs.gov.au for the most current evidence requirements.

column2: Can I do a student exchange while studying the BIT?
column3: UQ offers exchange and study abroad opportunities through its global programs. Check study.uq.edu.au for partner universities, eligibility, and how exchange units may count towards your BIT degree.

column2: What is UQ's policy on academic integrity — what happens if I'm caught cheating in a BIT course?
column3: UQ takes academic integrity seriously; breaches such as plagiarism or contract cheating can result in penalties ranging from a reduced grade to exclusion from the university. Full details are available at integrity.uq.edu.au and in the Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy at policies.uq.edu.au.

column2: I want to apply for BIT Honours starting Semester 1 — when is the deadline if I'm an international student?
column3: The application deadline for international students applying to BIT Honours commencing Semester 1 is 30 November. Domestic students have until 31 January for the same intake.

column2: What is the deadline to apply for BIT Honours if I want to start in Semester 2 and I'm a domestic student?
column3: Domestic students applying for BIT Honours with a Semester 2 commencement must submit their application by 30 June. International students have a deadline of 31 May for the same intake.

column2: If I'm studying BIT on a student visa and I fail some courses, could it affect my visa?
column3: Yes. Student visa holders must maintain satisfactory academic progress and comply with their Confirmation of Enrolment (CoE) conditions. Falling below satisfactory progress could result in a visa compliance issue, so contact UQ's Student Services if you are at risk.

column2: Does UQ use AI detection tools to check student assignments?
column3: UQ's academic integrity policies address the use of AI-generated content in assessments, and the university uses various tools to support this. For specific policies on AI use in BIT courses, refer to individual course profiles and integrity.uq.edu.au.

column2: Is the BIT recognised overseas, or is it mainly just useful in Australia?
column3: UQ's IT qualifications are internationally recognised, and graduates are able to work anywhere in the world according to the EAIT faculty. ACS accreditation also supports recognition within international ICT professional frameworks.

column2: I'm thinking about doing AI-related electives in my BIT — is there a specific AI major available?
column3: The BIT offers majors such as Computer Science, Data Science, and Software Engineering which may include AI-related content. For the specific list of available majors and their course content, check Programs and Courses at study.uq.edu.au.

column2: Can I study a BIT course fully online from overseas as an international student?
column3: UQ BIT programs are primarily designed for on-campus delivery in Brisbane. For information about any flexible or online study options and visa implications, contact UQ Admissions directly via study.uq.edu.au.

column2: How much should I budget per month for living expenses if I'm going to live off campus as an international student?
column3: Off-campus living costs are approximately AUD $2,026 to $4,151 per month depending on your lifestyle and location. If you prefer on-campus residential college accommodation with meals included, expect to budget AUD $2,635 to $4,168 per month.

column2: What's the difference between the BIT and BIT Honours — is Honours just an extra year?
column3: Yes, the Bachelor of Information Technology (Honours) is a separate one-year program that follows the completion of the standard 3-year BIT, requiring a minimum GPA of 5.0. It involves advanced research or project work and is available for both Semester 1 and Semester 2 intakes.
